Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 — Homicide
Homicide is the most serious offence a Garda will ever investigate. The investigation is run by a Senior
Investigating Officer (SIO) — typically a Detective Superintendent or Detective Inspector with NBCI support.
The first responder, however, is almost always a uniformed Garda whose actions in the first hour shape every
subsequent step. This chapter is written for the first responder and the investigating Garda working within the
SIO’s team.
The chapter covers murder, manslaughter (voluntary and involuntary), infanticide, and the operational realities
of homicide investigation: scene management, post-mortem, family liaison, suspect strategy, and the
courtroom-level rigour demanded by the Central Criminal Court.

1.0 Homicide — Foundations
Irish homicide law is a hybrid: the actus reus of murder and manslaughter is common law (causing death of
another); the mens rea distinction (intent to kill or cause serious injury) is partially codified in s.4 Criminal
Justice Act 1964; specific defences and modifications are statutory (provocation — common law; diminished
responsibility — s.6 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006; self-defence — s.18 NFOAP 1997 and s.2 Criminal
Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011).

Key Authority
DPP v Hull [1996] IECCA — Inferred Intent
Holding: Intent to kill or cause serious injury may be inferred from the nature of the act — weapon used, target
area, force, repetition.
Why It Matters: Build the inferential picture: weapon, target, repetition, force, suspect words / conduct before
and after.
The People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — Provocation
Holding: Provocation is a partial defence reducing murder to manslaughter. Subjective test — the accused’s
actual response — but the loss of self-control must be sudden and temporary.
Why It Matters: Document the timeline meticulously: provocation, response, cooling-off opportunities.
DPP v Davis [2001] 1 IR 146 — Provocation Refined
Holding: Re-stated MacEoin. Accused must have actually lost self-control; mere anger insufficient.
DPP v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — Self-Defence
Holding: Self-defence requires (a) honest belief in need to use force, and (b) reasonable use of force in the
circumstances as believed. Excessive self-defence reduces murder to manslaughter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Why It Matters: Investigate IP’s conduct toward suspect AND suspect’s response. Both central.
Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 — Statutory Self-Defence in Home
S.2 sets out a statutory framework for use of force in defence of one’s dwelling. Force such as the user
reasonably believed necessary; reasonable belief judged on circumstances as the user saw them; no duty to
retreat. Application: homeowner kills intruder — the 2011 Act may be the operative framework.
S.6 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006 — Diminished Responsibility
Reduces murder to manslaughter where the accused was suffering from a mental disorder substantially
impairing mental responsibility. Psychiatric evidence required.
DPP v Reilly [2004] IESC 51 — Joint Enterprise
Holding: Joint enterprise liability requires the accomplice to have foreseen the principal’s use of the means
causing death. Foresight, not intent, suffices.
Why It Matters: Multi-suspect cases turn on foresight. Build role-by-role evidence.
ECHR Article 2 — Right to Life (Procedural Obligation)
McCann v UK (1995), Jordan v UK (2001), Finucane v UK (2003) — Article 2 imposes a procedural obligation
on the State to investigate deaths effectively, independently, promptly, and with public scrutiny. Underpins the
SIO regime. Procedural shortcuts can be challenged at Strasbourg level.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 1 — suspects in homicide investigation in custody at maximum salience; full safeguards.
• Std 4 — family of deceased treated with respect regardless of relationship between deceased and family.
• Std 9 — every step must withstand SIO review, DPP scrutiny, court challenge, Coroner’s inquiry, possible
Strasbourg review.

1.1 Murder
MURDER: The unlawful killing of another person with intent to kill or cause serious injury. Common
law offence; mens rea partially codified by s.4 Criminal Justice Act 1964. Mandatory life sentence.

The Three Elements
6. Unlawful killing — caused by the accused’s act, not justified or excused at law.
7. Of another person — a human being recognised at law.
8. With intent to kill or cause serious injury (s.4 CJA 1964) — not mere recklessness.

Track A — Live Scene
First 60 Minutes — The Most Important Hour
9. Approach with safety in mind — suspect on scene? Weapons / firearms? Other casualties?
10. Life — first aid until paramedics arrive. If deceased confirmed by paramedics, note time and paramedic
name. Do not move the body.
11. Suspect — if on scene, arrest under s.4 CJA 1984. Search for weapons. Preserve clothing for forensic.
PROOF check.
12. Cordon — outer cordon broad, inner cordon at scene perimeter, sterile zone immediately around the
deceased.
13. Common Approach Path — single defined route in and out. ELOG starts now.
14. Photograph — the scene as found, before any movement, before SOCO arrival. Wide / mid / close.
15. Witnesses — identify, segregate (independent accounts), keep on scene for SIO arrival or escort to station.
16. Notify — within minutes: Sergeant, District Officer, Detective Inspector / Superintendent, NBCI on-call.
17. Notebook — contemporaneous, detailed, sequenced. Becomes the foundation document. Every
observation, word spoken to you, action taken — captured.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

THE FIRST GARDA AT A MURDER SCENE
Your single most important contribution is preservation. The forensic, witness, and continuity foundation of the
entire case is laid in the first sixty minutes. SIO, NBCI, the courts, and the family depend on your discipline.
Do not enter the scene further than necessary to confirm death and check for further casualties. Do not let anyone
else enter who does not need to. Do not touch the body or surroundings.
Your notebook entry of this hour will be exhibited in the Central Criminal Court.

SIO Arrival and Handover
Once an SIO is appointed (typically within the first hour), responsibility passes upwards. The first responder
briefs the SIO, hands over the scene log, takes direction. The investigating Garda working as part of the SIO
team takes specific tasks: witness statements, exhibit handling, CCTV, family liaison, suspect interviews,
depending on assignment.
The SIO will typically activate:
• Major Investigation Team (MIT) structure
• Scenes of Crime Officer (SOCO) deployment and Garda Technical Bureau (GTB)
• Office of the State Pathologist (post-mortem)
• Coroner notification (mandatory in suspicious death)
• Forensic Science Ireland submissions
• Family Liaison Officer (FLO)
• Garda Press Office for media liaison
• PULSE Major Incident Room operations

Scene Management — Homicide Protocol
Cordon
• Outer cordon — manage public and media; consider broader than initial instinct.
• Inner cordon — perimeter of the immediate scene; only SOCO and SIO-authorised personnel.
• Sterile zone — immediate vicinity of the body; only pathologist, designated SOCO, SIO.
Common Approach Path (CAP)
• Single defined route from cordon to sterile zone; protected from contamination.
ELOG
• Every person crossing the outer cordon: time in, time out, role, purpose.
• Maintained throughout life of the scene (which may be days).
Photographic Record
• Pre-SOCO record — captures as-found state.
• Full SOCO photography — 360 degrees, multi-scale, reference markers.
• Body in situ before removal; same after removal showing position relative to scene.
Body Removal
• Only on State Pathologist authority.
• Photographed in situ; bagged; chain-of-custody to mortuary.

Evidence Targets
• Body — post-mortem by State Pathologist; cause of death; toxicology; wounds and mechanism.
• Weapon — if recovered, full forensic submission (prints, DNA, biological transfer, ballistic if firearm).
• Clothing — of deceased, suspect, possibly witnesses. Preserved separately.
• Scene forensic — fingerprints, DNA, blood pattern, trace evidence.
• CCTV — broad bracket; multiple sources; approach, event, egress.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Phone / digital — deceased’s phone, suspect’s phone, communications, location data.
• Vehicles — ANPR; full vehicle forensics.
• Witnesses — each account preserved in detail. May be the only basis for prosecution.
• Medical history of deceased — GP records, hospital records, prior conditions; Coroner liaison.

Post-Mortem
Conducted by the State Pathologist or Deputy at City Mortuary, Dublin. Attended by:
• Investigating Garda (usually designated Detective)
• SOCO (for evidence recovery from body — clothing, samples, scrapings)
• Pathologist’s assistant
• Garda photographer
Outputs: pathologist’s report, toxicology (separate, weeks later), histology (separate, weeks later), exhibits
recovered. The pathologist’s evidence is central at trial.

Family Liaison
Family of deceased entitled to information, dignity, and a single named point of contact — the FLO. The FLO’s
role is dual: support the family AND investigate (family often the most important witnesses in domestic
homicide). FLO operates under SIO direction; family privacy balanced against investigative need; sensitive
information (autopsy details, suspect identification timing, court timing) managed deliberately.
COMPLIMENT POINT — FAMILY LIAISON
The FLO who carries a family through the months between death and trial — explaining the post-mortem, the
bail process, the inquest, the trial — does work that does not appear in the file but underpins the entire process.
Action: written compliment in cover report; consider formal commendation submission for sustained exceptional
engagement.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Murder is arrestable. S.4 CJA 1984 (12 hours, Superintendent-extensible to 24). S.50 CJA 2007 available (up
to 168 hours in stages with senior officer authorisations) where murder connected to organised crime.
Specimen arrest words: “I am Garda [name] from Mountjoy. I am arresting you under section 4 of the
Criminal Justice Act 1984 on suspicion of the murder of [deceased name] said to have occurred at [location]
on [date]. You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken
down in writing and may be given in evidence.”

Custody and Interview
Interview Detective-led, two-person teams in rotation. Features distinct from lower-level offences:
• Mandatory legal access (Salduz / Doyle / Gormley).
• Full audio / video recording.
• Multiple sessions across detention; rest, refreshment, lawyer access between.
• Inference warnings carefully considered (s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984).
• Topic-disciplined sessions — not unstructured fishing.
Topics typically include:
18. Relationship with deceased.
19. Movements before, during, after time of death.
20. Account of events at scene.
21. Specific evidence put — forensic, CCTV, witness, post-mortem.
22. Weapon — origin, fate.
23. Mental state — carefully explored if partial defences in play.
24. Inference questions where applicable.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PULSE / PEMS / File
PULSE category: Homicide — Murder.
Major Incident structure: Central exhibit register, witness register, suspect register, action register, decision
log.
File: Indictment-ready file built throughout investigation, not at end; SIO directs file build via investigating
Garda team.
DPP submission: Via Detective Superintendent / Chief Superintendent through DPP’s Directing Division.

Specimen Wording
Notebook Entry — First Responder
“At [time] on [date] I received a report via [source] of [event] at [location]. I arrived at [time]. On arrival I
observed [scene state in detail]. I confirmed [scene actions]. I spoke with [persons — statements verbatim]. I
made the following decisions: [list with rationale]. I notified [chain] at [times]. I commenced scene log / ELOG
at [time].”
Statement Opening — Witness
“At approximately [time] on [date] I was [activity] at [location]. I observed / heard / felt [account]. I provide
this statement of my own free will. I am aware that I may be required to give evidence in court.”
Memo of Interview — Opening
“Interview commenced [time]. Present: D/Garda [name], D/Garda [name], suspect [name], solicitor [name].
The suspect was cautioned: [exact form]. Suspect acknowledged understanding. Audio and video recording
confirmed running at [time]. Solicitor consultation: [time started — time ended].”

Defence Will Attack
Typical Attack Vectors
25. Identification — alibi in single-suspect cases; joint-enterprise scope in multi-suspect.
26. Causation — pre-existing medical condition; intervening medical treatment; eggshell-skull; multi-causal.
27. Mens rea — accused intended assault, not death or serious injury (manslaughter route).
28. Partial defences — provocation (MacEoin / Davis), excessive self-defence (Dwyer), diminished
responsibility (s.6 Insanity Act 2006).
29. Full defences — self-defence (common law / s.2 CL(D&D)A 2011); consent (very limited); accident.
30. Custody admissibility — lawyer access, oppression, recording integrity.
31. Disclosure — incomplete or delayed; conviction-ending.
32. Forensic chain — every step probed.
33. Joint enterprise — individual accused’s knowledge / foresight.

Appellate Authority Where Defence Won
• DPP v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — provocation framework.
• DPP v Davis [2001] 1 IR 146 — provocation refinement.
• DPP v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — self-defence reduction.
• DPP v Reilly [2004] IESC 51 — joint enterprise foresight.
• DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 — exclusionary rule.
• DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — warrant validity.
• DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 / DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 / Salduz v Turkey [GC] 2008 —
custody lawyer access.
• McCann v UK (1995) / Finucane v UK (2003) / Jordan v UK (2001) — Article 2 procedural standards.
How to Bullet-Proof
• Identification: cumulative — CCTV, forensic, witness, communications, presence at scene.
• Causation: pathologist detailed; complications addressed; chain documented from injury to death.
• Mens rea: build inferentially from weapon, target, force, repetition, conduct.
• Partial defences: probe in interview; document suspect’s state of mind.
• Self-defence: investigate IP’s role; third-party statements; suspect’s account tested.
• Custody: rigorous documentation; recording integrity; lawyer access; rest periods.
• Disclosure: comprehensive schedule maintained by SIO disclosure officer; revisited before every key step.
• Forensic: SOCO statements detailed; transfer chains; lab provenance.
• Joint enterprise: role-by-role evidence (communications, position, post-incident conduct).

Red Flag Escalation
Murder is itself the highest escalation tier. Within homicide:
• NBCI involvement — standard for any homicide outside the most clear-cut.
• Special Detective Unit / GNDOCB — organised-crime homicide.
• Ballistic Section of GTB — firearms-involved.
• Forensic Science Ireland — toxicology, DNA, firearms residue, blood pattern, document.
• State Pathologist’s Office — mandatory.
• Coroner liaison — mandatory; inquest in parallel with criminal process.
• Garda Press Office — media strategy; family considerations; public appeal for information.

Common Pitfalls
• Contamination of scene by responding personnel — even necessary contact must be documented and
minimised.
• Failing to caution suspect at right moment — admissible vs inadmissible admissions turns on this.
• Inadequate first-responder notebook — the foundational document.
• Family liaison failures — disengagement reduces investigative quality.
• Disclosure lapses — catastrophic; conviction-ending.
• Premature media disclosure of investigative detail.
• Failing to preserve suspect clothing and biological samples promptly.
• Over-charging or under-charging at indictment stage.

Ethics Touchpoints
• Std 1 — maximum salience. Suspect custody safeguards comprehensive.
• Std 2 — arrest grounds carefully articulated; search authorities lawful and proportionate.
• Std 3 — sensitive information strictly controlled.
• Std 4 — family of deceased treated with consistent respect; suspect treated within Code regardless of public

sentiment.
• Std 7 — disclosure obligations especially. Speak up on lapses.
• Std 8 — every decision documented; SIO decision log open to scrutiny.
• Std 9 — every step capable of withstanding Strasbourg-level review under Article 2.

1.2 Manslaughter
MANSLAUGHTER: The unlawful killing of another person without the mens rea for murder. Common
law offence. Discretionary life sentence.

Two Forms
Voluntary Manslaughter
Murder mens rea present, but a partial defence reduces:
• Provocation (MacEoin, Davis)
• Excessive self-defence (Dwyer)
• Diminished responsibility (s.6 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006)
Involuntary Manslaughter
Murder mens rea absent; death from one of two bases:
• Unlawful and dangerous act — accused performed an unlawful act (assault, criminal damage etc.) that
was objectively dangerous; death resulted. Mens rea is mens rea of the underlying act. Common in “one
punch” killings.
• Gross negligence — duty of care; breach amounting to gross negligence; obvious and serious risk of death;
death caused. Often in vehicle, workplace, care-of-vulnerable settings.

Key Authority
DPP v Mahalingam [1995] 2 IR 117 — Unlawful Act Manslaughter
Holding: Unlawful act must be objectively dangerous — a sober and reasonable person would recognise the
risk of some harm. The accused need not have appreciated the risk.
Why It Matters: Highly relevant to one-punch cases. Objective dangerousness, not subjective appreciation.
DPP v Curran [2011] IECCA — Gross Negligence Manslaughter
Holding: Requires (a) duty of care, (b) breach amounting to gross negligence, (c) breach creating a serious and
obvious risk of death, (d) breach causing death.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Why It Matters: Evidence of each limb required. Often multi-agency (HSA, regulators).

Investigation — Particular Features
• Mens rea more often the live issue — expect defence to fight on intent. Build inferential picture thoroughly.
• Provocation, self-defence, diminished responsibility frequently in play — IP-suspect history central.
• Single-blow cases require detailed mechanism evidence — pathologist on precise cause.
• Gross-negligence cases require duty-and-breach evidence — often involves multiple agencies.

Charge Selection
In most cases, charge is murder, with manslaughter argued as alternative by defence (or accepted via plea). In
gross-negligence cases, manslaughter is primary. The DPP’s charging directions are usually staged.

Defence Will Attack
• Mens rea grade — even manslaughter requires the mens rea of the underlying unlawful act.
• Causation — intervening medical treatment, pre-existing condition.
• Voluntary act — accident defence.
• Self-defence (full or partial).
• Causation in gross-negligence cases — was the breach the cause of death.

1.3 Infanticide
INFANTICIDE: The killing of a child under 12 months by its mother, where at the time the balance of
her mind was disturbed by reason of not having fully recovered from giving birth, or the effect of
lactation. Infanticide Act 1949. Treated as manslaughter for sentence.
Rare and deeply tragic. Investigation led by senior officers in collaboration with Tusla, mental health services,
social work. First-responder priority: surviving family, deceased child, scene, welfare of any other children in
household. Statements approached with extreme care — mother likely in acute mental distress.

Key Considerations
• Forensic determination of cause of death — distinguishing from cot death, natural, accidental.
• Mental health history of mother — GP, perinatal mental health team, hospital.
• Family circumstances — partner, other children, social context.
• Charges may be infanticide ab initio, or murder / manslaughter with infanticide as fallback. DPP direction
led by psychiatric evidence.

Ethics Touchpoints

• Std 1 — mother as suspect with acute mental health needs; appropriate adult; psychiatric assessment; pause
where necessary.
• Std 4 — dignified treatment notwithstanding the offence.
• Std 3 — absolute confidentiality; media liaison especially sensitive.

Chapter 2 — Assault
Assault is the highest-volume person-crime category. Mountjoy district sees the full spectrum: minor publichouse pushes, drunken street altercations on Talbot Street, serious assaults with bladed weapons in flat
complexes, sustained domestic violence, and a steady stream of assaults on Gardaí in the course of duty. The
Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 (NFOAP) is the central statute. Three principal offences form
a ladder: s.2 (minor), s.3 (causing harm), s.4 (causing serious harm).

2.0 Assault — Foundations
ASSAULT (foundational): A person assaults another where, without lawful excuse, intentionally or
recklessly: (a) directly or indirectly applies force to or causes an impact on the body of another, or (b)
causes another to believe on reasonable grounds that he or she is likely to be subjected to such force.
NFOAP 1997 s.2 (foundational definition adopted across the assault ladder).

Key Concepts
• Force — any contact (s.2(2)). Hostility is not required; the force must be without consent.
• Causes another to believe — the psychic assault. No contact required; threat creating apprehension
suffices.
• Without lawful excuse — self-defence, defence of others, defence of property, reasonable parental
chastisement (substantially narrowed by s.28 Children First Act 2015), and consent in specific contexts.
• Harm — harm to body or mind, including pain and unconsciousness (s.1).
• Serious harm — injury creating a substantial risk of death or causing serious disfigurement, or substantial
loss or impairment of mobility of body as a whole or of the function of any particular bodily member or
organ (s.1).

Key Authority
DPP v Murray [1977] IR 360 — Recklessness (subjective)
Already cited. Recklessness for s.2 / s.3 / s.4 NFOAP requires subjective awareness of the risk.
DPP v Cagney and McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111 — Recklessness Reaffirmed
Re-stated the Murray standard. Particularly important for s.4 serious-harm charges where the prosecution relies
on recklessness.
DPP v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — Self-Defence
Already cited (Ch.1). Applies directly to assault: honest belief in need to use force + reasonable force in the
circumstances as believed.
R v Brown [1994] 1 AC 212 (UK — persuasive on consent)
Holding: Consent is not a defence to actual bodily harm or above where the activity is not within recognised
exceptions (sport, surgery, body modification within limits, etc.).
Why It Matters: Consent defence in assault is narrow. Particularly important in DV cases where defence may
claim consensual roughness.
DPP v Murphy [2005] IECCA — Direct and Indirect Force
Holding: Force may be applied directly (a blow) or indirectly (setting a dog, throwing an object, pulling a chair
away). The act need not be the immediate cause of the contact.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 4 — intoxicated IP / suspect; same evidential rigour regardless of intoxication.
• Std 1 — medical assessment of suspect on arrival; possible mental health, intoxication.
• Std 7 — colleague who has been assaulted in course of duty: maintain investigative discipline.

2.1 Assault (Minor) — s.2 NFOAP 1997
ASSAULT (s.2): The foundational assault offence (definition above). No requirement of harm. Contrary
to s.2 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 6 months / Class C fine on summary conviction. Summary only.

Track A — Live
First 60 Minutes
34. Separate the parties — first action. Public-order risk; further injury risk.
35. Medical assessment of IP and any other injured person.
36. Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984 only if the offence in contemplation is arrestable (s.3 or s.4 NFOAP, not s.2
alone). For s.2 only, arrest is under common law breach-of-peace or by summons proceeding.
37. Witness identification at scene.
38. Notebook — verbatim of IP’s and any witness’s first account; suspect’s words; observable behaviour of
both parties.
39. Photograph injuries / scene — with consent where IP.
S.2 vs Higher — The Charge Question
The charge depends on the harm caused (or not caused). Document the injuries precisely. A red mark is s.2; a
bruise that needs no treatment is borderline s.2/s.3; an injury requiring hospital is s.3; a permanent or lifethreatening injury is s.4. The Garda’s observation, IP’s description, and medical evidence between them
determine the charge tier.

Track B — Post-Fact Report
First Actions
40. Calm account from IP — timeline, location, suspect, mechanism.
41. Medical follow-up if any injury — GP / A&E records.
42. Photograph injuries — even days later, bruising may still be visible and develop further.
43. Witness identification — anyone present, anyone IP confided in.
44. CCTV — within shift.
45. Communications — messages between IP and suspect before / after.

Evidence Targets
• IP statement — mechanism, suspect identification, injuries, witnesses.
• Photographs of injuries — at multiple time points (initial, 48 hours, 7 days where appropriate).
• Medical evidence — GP letter, A&E discharge summary, treatment records.
• Witness statements.
• CCTV.
• Communications evidence — texts, social media, voicemails.

Suspect Strategy
S.2 is summary only and not arrestable for the purpose of s.4 CJA 1984 detention. Most cases proceed by
summons. Arrest only on the basis of common-law breach-of-peace power or where the offence may upgrade
to s.3 (in which case arrest under s.4 CJA 1984 on the s.3 footing).

Interview Plan
Often by appointment for s.2. If interviewed:
46. Account of the incident.
47. Relationship with IP.
48. Self-defence / defence of property / lawful excuse claimed.
49. Specific evidence put (witnesses, CCTV, injuries).

Statement Opening — IP

Defence Will Attack
Typical Attack Vectors
50. Self-defence — “My client was responding to the IP’s aggression.”
51. Lawful excuse — ejecting from premises, defence of another, defence of property.
52. No force / no apprehension — contact incidental, no real fear.
53. Consent — horseplay or sport context.
54. IP reliability — intoxication, motive (e.g. ongoing dispute).
Appellate Authority
• DPP v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — self-defence.
• DPP v Murphy [2005] IECCA — direct / indirect force.
• R v Brown [1994] 1 AC 212 — consent narrow.
How to Bullet-Proof
• IP statement on absence of consent / provocation.
• Witness statements on sequence — who started, who hit, in what order.
• CCTV continuity from before the altercation.
• Suspect interview on claimed defence — test thoroughly.

Red Flag Escalation

protections.

2.2 Assault Causing Harm — s.3 NFOAP 1997
ASSAULT CAUSING HARM: Assault (s.2 definition) causing harm to the IP. “Harm” means harm to
body or mind, including pain and unconsciousness (s.1). Contrary to s.3 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 5
years on indictment. Either District or Circuit on DPP direction.
The bread-and-butter assault charge for serious injuries short of life-threatening. Typical Mountjoy
presentations: broken nose, fractured cheekbone, broken hand, lacerations requiring stitches, kicking-injuries,
glass-injuries. Many DV assaults are charged at s.3 level.

Track A and Track B — As s.2 but with Medical Priority
Investigation broadly as s.2 minor assault, with three particular features:
• Medical evidence is central — hospital records, GP letter, treating professional’s statement on injuries,
mechanism, treatment, prognosis.
• Photographic record more important — bruising develops, scarring sets; photograph at multiple time points.
• Witness statement on mechanism (the IP’s account of how they were injured) is the evidential bridge
between assault and harm.

Suspect Strategy
S.3 is arrestable (max 5 yrs). S.4 CJA 1984 detention available. Full PROOF check.
Arrest words: “I am Garda [name] from Mountjoy. I am arresting you under section 4 of the Criminal Justice
Act 1984 on suspicion of assault causing harm contrary to section 3 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the
Person Act 1997, said to have occurred at [location] on [date]. You are not obliged to say anything unless you
wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”

55. Movements; presence at scene; relationship with IP.
56. Account of the incident — the suspect’s sequence.
57. Specific acts — each blow / push / contact separately.
58. Injuries — awareness, intent.
59. Self-defence / defence of others / property — probe.
60. Forensic and CCTV evidence put.

Typical Vectors
62. Causation — was the injury caused by the suspect’s act or some other event (fall, prior injury, third party)?
63. Identification — chaotic scenes, multiple participants.
64. Self-defence / excessive self-defence — the most common defence.
65. Mens rea — intent or recklessness; sometimes attacked on “did not realise risk” basis.

Appellate Authority

How to Bullet-Proof
• Medical evidence: treating-professional statement specifying mechanism (“injury consistent with blunt
force trauma to the face”) and ruling out alternative causes where possible.
• IP statement: detailed sequence; mechanism in IP’s own words; absence of consent.
• Witness corroboration.
• Suspect interview: probe self-defence thoroughly; document the IP’s alleged aggressive conduct.
• Photographic record of injuries with scale reference.

Red Flag Escalation
• DV context — DPSU brief; safety plan; protection-order consideration.
• Series / pattern — District tasking.
• Hate element — hate-crime tag.
• Vulnerable IP.

• Charging s.2 because “IP only had bruises” — pain alone is harm under s.1. The s.3 charge is correct.
• Inadequate medical evidence — the prosecution needs treating-professional evidence, not just IP
description.
• Photographing only at first attendance — bruising and scarring evolve.

2.3 Assault Causing Serious Harm — s.4 NFOAP 1997

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

ASSAULT CAUSING SERIOUS HARM: Assault causing injury creating a substantial risk of death, OR
causing serious disfigurement, OR causing substantial loss or impairment of mobility of body as a whole
or of function of a particular bodily member or organ. Contrary to s.4 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: Life.
The most serious non-fatal offence. Stabbings (where IP survives), severe beatings, gunshot wounds, acid
attacks, broken bones with permanent functional loss. Inner-city Dublin contexts: feud-related stabbings, severe
DV assaults, public-order escalations into serious assault, attacks on healthcare / transport workers, assaults
during burglary or robbery (which may be additionally charged as aggravated burglary / robbery).

Track A — Live
First 60 Minutes
67. Life-saving — first aid, pressure on bleeding, airway, recovery position. The Garda’s response in this
minute may be the difference between s.4 and homicide.
68. Ambulance — 999 / 112. Hospital transport priority.
69. Suspect — arrest under s.4 CJA 1984; possibly s.50 CJA 2007 if organised feature.
70. Cordon — the scene is a forensic priority; full SOCO posture.
71. Weapon — photograph in situ; SOCO recovery; chain of custody.
72. IP’s clothing — preserve, sometimes via hospital.
73. Detective Unit / Superintendent brief immediately.
COMPLIMENT POINT — LIFE-SAVING FIRST AID
Where a Garda’s first-aid intervention prevented a fatal outcome — pressure on bleeding, airway maintenance,
CPR, recovery position — the contribution should be formally recognised.
Action: detailed account in Garda’s own statement, commendation submission, brief to Superintendent. Many
such interventions go unnamed and merit recognition both for the colleague’s record and for institutional culture.

Scene Management
As homicide (Ch.1) but with the IP alive. Full forensic posture. Cordon, CAP, ELOG. Hospital scene also
engaged — IP’s clothing as exhibit; medical staff as witnesses to injury, mechanism described by IP, treatment
given.

• Medical evidence — hospital records, surgical reports, statement of treating consultant on injuries,

• IP statement — sometimes initially limited by medical condition; supplementary statements as condition

• CCTV — broad.
• Forensic at scene — SOCO.
• Forensic on body / clothing — of suspect and IP; transfer evidence.
• Phone / digital.
• Witnesses — each separately.

S.4 CJA 1984 detention. Possible s.50 CJA 2007 detention with senior officer authorisation in organised
contexts. Detective-led investigation.

AN GARDA SÍOCHÁNA
DMR North Central — Mountjoy Garda Station

THE INJURED PARTY STATEMENT
Assault (s.2) & Assault Causing Harm (s.3) — Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act
1997
An investigator’s deep guide: what to capture, what to probe, and every situation to think about

1

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

How to use this guide
This is a working aid for taking the injured party’s statement in an assault investigation — the
single most important document in most assault files. A statement that proves the elements,
captures the harm, forecloses the defences and preserves the evidence usually decides the
case; a thin statement rarely recovers later.
The guide runs in the order you actually work: the offence you are proving (so you know what the
statement must establish), the preliminaries before you write a word, the recommended
structure, then a deep prompt bank organised by topic, situation-specific playbooks, the
photographs/medical piece, an element-by-element checklist, and a model skeleton. The green
boxes are prompts to get you thinking; use the ones that fit.
A NOTE ON ROLES. The injured party is a witness, not a suspect — no caution is administered
to a witness. But if there is any prospect the injured party was also a participant (a mutual fight),
pause and consider their status before proceeding (see the playbook on mutual fights).
CURRENCY. The s.3 penalty was increased by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Act 2023; the same Act introduced a distinct offence of non-fatal strangulation/suffocation
(s.3A), and the Criminal Justice (Hate Offences) Act 2024 created assault offences aggravated
by hatred. Verify the current penalties, commencement and charging options on
revisedacts.lawreform.ie before relying.

2

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

1. The offence you are proving
Take the statement with the elements in front of you. Every element is something the injured
party’s account should help establish. Decide early whether you are looking at a s.2 (no or
minimal injury) or s.3 (harm) — but take the fuller statement regardless; the injury picture often
develops.

s.2(3) — THE DE MINIMIS POINT. s.2 is not committed where the force is of a kind generally
acceptable in ordinary daily life (the incidental bump). This is rarely relevant to a real assault, but
the statement should make clear the contact was not trivial or accidental.

1.2 Where it sits on the ladder
Keep the whole ladder in mind, because the injuries may take the case up or down it: s.2 assault
(no/minimal injury) → s.3 assault causing harm (harm to body or mind) → s.4 causing serious
harm (substantial risk of death, serious disfigurement, or substantial loss/impairment of function).
Consider also s.3A non-fatal strangulation/suffocation (no physical harm need be shown),
threats to kill/cause serious harm (s.5), and the hate-aggravated forms. The statement should
capture enough that the correct charge can be selected and can move up if the injuries prove
worse than they first looked.

3

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Before you start: which section fits what you know now? What
would move it up (a fracture on X-ray, loss of consciousness, hand to the throat) or down? Take
the statement so the file can go either way.

4

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

2. Before you write a word — preliminaries
• Welfare and medical first. Health before statement. If the IP needs an ambulance, hospital
or first aid, that comes first — and the fact of it is itself evidence of harm. A short first
account can be noted; the full statement can follow when they are fit.
• Capacity and condition. Are they intoxicated, concussed, in shock, in severe pain or
medicated? Note their condition; consider whether to take a short account now and a full
statement later when they can give a reliable, considered account.
• Safety and domestic context. Is the suspect still nearby? Is this domestic? Is the IP safe
to speak freely, and safe after you leave? Separate them from the suspect and from anyone
who might influence the account.
• Victim’s rights. Give the information victims are entitled to (the Victims of Crime Act 2017 /
Garda victim service): what happens next, updates, support services, and the option of a
Victim Impact Statement later.
• Vulnerability and special measures. Is the IP a child, elderly, disabled, or intimidated?
Consider specialist interviewers, an intermediary, video-recording, and later trial special
measures (screen, live link, no personal cross-examination).
• Interpreter. If English is not their first language, use an accredited interpreter — not a
family member, and never the suspect’s associate. Record who interpreted.
• The first account is evidence. What the IP said to the first person (a 999 call, a bystander,
the first Garda) may be admissible as a recent complaint or res gestae. Capture who they
first told and their exact words, and preserve the 999 recording.
• Photographs and medical consent. Plan early to photograph injuries and to obtain
consent for a medical report/records. Bruising develops — plan follow-up photographs at
24–48 hours.
• Format and integrity. Take it in the IP’s own words, first person, in sequence, without
leading. Read it back, let them correct it, and have them adopt it in the standard form.
Record time, date, place and who was present.
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Is this the right moment and place for the full statement? A quiet,
safe setting with the IP calm and sober produces a far stronger account than a doorway at 3 a.m.
Note a first account now; take the full statement when conditions allow — and record why.

5

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

3. The statement — recommended structure
A statement that follows a logical spine is easier to give, harder to attack, and quicker to prove in
court. Suggested order:
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Who the injured party is — identity, and how they come to be giving the statement.
The relationship to the suspect and any relevant history.
The setting — date, time, place, conditions, who was present.
The lead-up — what was happening immediately before.
The assault — the core, blow by blow, in sequence, in their own words.
Words spoken — before, during and after.
Identification — how they know / can identify the suspect.
The injuries and harm — physical and psychological, immediate and continuing.
The aftermath — first complaint, who was called, what they did.
Evidence — clothing, CCTV, phone footage, witnesses, medical.
Consent, willingness and impact — no consent to the force; willing to prosecute and attend;
consent to photos and medical release; impact.

6

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

4. The deep prompt bank
Work through the topics that fit. Not every prompt applies to every case — but reading them will
surface the questions a thin statement misses. Ask open questions first (“tell me what
happened”), then close down the detail.

4.A The injured party
• Full name, date of birth, address, occupation, contact details; any reason they may be hard
to contact for trial.
• How they come to be making the statement; their role that day; whether sober and well
when it happened.

4.B Relationship to the suspect and history
• Do they know the suspect? How, how long, by what name, nickname, address? (This
anchors identification — see 4.G.)
• Any prior incidents, threats, or violence between them — dates, what happened, any
previous reports, any protective/barring orders.
• Any motive the IP is aware of — a dispute, debt, jealousy, previous row; any ongoing
intimidation.
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: If they know the suspect, is there a history that turns a ‘one-off’ into
a pattern? Previous unreported incidents, prior injuries, prior threats — all relevant to context,
bad-character applications, and (in a domestic) risk.

4.C The setting
•
•
•
•

Exact location (address / premises / part of the street); indoors or out; the layout.
Date and time, as precise as possible; how they fix the time (an event, a call, a clock).
Lighting, weather, noise; how well they could see and hear.
Who else was present — names, descriptions, where each person was and what they could
see.

4.D The lead-up
• What was happening immediately before — minutes and seconds matter.
• Any argument, words, threats or gestures; who said what; who approached whom.
• Alcohol or drugs — the IP’s and, as observed, the suspect’s (relevant to recklessness and
reliability).
• Was any weapon or object visible or mentioned before the assault?
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Who was the aggressor? Nail this down early and specifically — it
is the heart of any self-defence claim. Establish that the IP did not start it, or had withdrawn,
before the force was used on them (see 4.K).

4.E The assault — the core, blow by blow
This is the element. Slow the account right down and take it in sequence. Do not let “he hit me a
few times” stand — break it out.
• The mechanism of each act: punch, slap, kick, stamp, headbutt, push, choke, bite, spit,
thrown object, weapon.
• How many times; the sequence; which came first; how much time it took.
• Which part of the suspect’s body (fist, boot, head) struck which part of the IP’s body.
• Force used — in the IP’s own words (“as hard as he could”, “full force”); the effect of each
blow.

7

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

• The IP’s position at each stage — standing, falling, on the ground; were they held,
restrained, cornered, or trapped?
• Were they defenceless, trying to protect themselves, trying to get away? (Defensive posture
rebuts consent and self-defence by the suspect.)
• Any weapon or object: describe it, where it came from, where it went, how it was used,
contact made.
• Any pressure to the neck / hands to the throat / difficulty breathing / feeling faint — capture
this precisely (see the strangulation playbook).
• Spitting or biting — where, and any bodily fluid contact (infection/forensic considerations).
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Reconstruct it like a slow-motion film: where were the hands, feet,
body of each person at each moment? A blow-by-blow account is what proves intent, defeats ‘it
was an accident’, and lets the injuries be matched to the mechanism.

4.F Words spoken — before, during, after
• Exact words the suspect used — threats, insults, motive (“I’ll kill you”, naming a grievance),
any admission.
• Words the IP used — asking them to stop, saying no, calling for help.
• Anything said afterwards — threats not to report, apologies, admissions to others.
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Words carry intent, motive, identification and sometimes a
confession. Capture them verbatim, in quotation marks, and note who else heard them — those
hearers may prove them and may support a res gestae or admission argument.

4.G Identification
How the IP ties the act to this person is often the fight. Take it differently depending on whether
the suspect is known.

Known suspect
• How they know them, by what name and address; how sure they are it was that person;
how well they saw them during the incident.
Stranger
• A full first description before any name is known: build, height, age, hair, facial hair,
complexion, distinguishing marks/tattoos, clothing, footwear, accent, anything distinctive.
• How long they had the person in view, at what distance, in what lighting; whether their
attention was on the face.
• Whether they could recognise the person again, and the basis for it (a specific feature or
action).
• Any CCTV, doorman, taxi, or third party who can assist identification; consideration of a
formal identification procedure.
MIND THE ID LAW. Honest witnesses can be mistaken. Anchor identification in prior
knowledge, a recorded first description, and the viewing conditions — not the eventual sight of
the accused. Consider a formal identification procedure rather than relying on a later informal or
dock identification.

4.H Injuries and harm — body and mind
For s.3, harm is the element that lifts the case above common assault — and “harm” expressly
includes harm to the mind, pain and unconsciousness. Capture all of it.

Physical
• Every injury, one by one: exact location on the body, type (bruise, cut, swelling, laceration,
fracture, loss of tooth, burn), size, and the pain at the time and since.
• Loss of consciousness — for how long, and who observed it (a serious marker of harm).

8

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

• Immediate vs developing: what appeared later (bruising often darkens over 24–48 hours) —
plan follow-up photographs.
• Medical attention: ambulance, ED/hospital, GP, dentist; treatment given; X-rays/scans;
stitches; medication; time in hospital.
• Ongoing and permanent effects: scarring, disfigurement, reduced movement, headaches,
numbness, dental damage.

Psychological (harm to the mind)
• Fear, shock, panic; sleep disturbance, nightmares, flashbacks; anxiety about going out;
avoidance of the location or the suspect.
• Any counselling, GP visits, medication, time off work, impact on family and daily life.
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Do not stop at the visible bruise. ‘Harm’ includes harm to the mind,
pain and unconsciousness — so a victim badly frightened, unable to sleep, or knocked out may
satisfy s.3 even where the marks are slight. Ask about the mind as carefully as the body, and get
the medical evidence to support it.

4.I Aftermath and first complaint
• What the IP did immediately after; who they told first and their exact words (recent
complaint / res gestae).
• Who called the Gardaí / ambulance and when; preserve the 999 call.
• What the suspect did after — fled, stayed, threatened, was detained by others.

4.J Evidence and continuity
•
•
•
•
•

Clothing worn (blood, tears, marks) — seize and preserve; do not let it be washed.
The weapon or object — location and preservation.
CCTV — premises, street, transport, doorbell; identify and preserve before it is overwritten.
Phone footage / photos taken by the IP or bystanders; social-media posts.
Other witnesses — names and accounts; the treating doctor for a medical
statement/records.

INVESTIGATOR — THINK: What is perishable? CCTV overwrites, bruises fade, clothing gets
washed, phones get wiped. List every perishable exhibit in the statement and act on preservation
the same day.

4.K Foreclosing the defences
Anticipate the defence and take the account that answers it now, while the IP is fresh.
• Self-defence / who started it. Establish the aggressor, that the IP did not initiate or had
withdrawn, and that the suspect’s force was not a proportionate response to any threat from
the IP.
• Consent. Make explicit that the IP did not agree to be struck; if the defence is ‘an agreed
fight’, address it directly.
• Accident. The blow-by-blow mechanism (4.E) and aimed blows rebut ‘it was an accident’.
• Provocation / ‘they asked for it’. Record what was actually said and done — provocation
is not a defence to assault, but the facts matter.
• Identification. See 4.G — the stronger the ID basis, the weaker this attack.

4.L Consent, willingness and impact
• A clear line that the IP did not consent to the force.
• Willingness to prosecute and to attend court; consent to injury photographs; consent to
release of medical records / a medical report.
• Impact — for the statement and for a later Victim Impact Statement; any fears and
protection needs.

9

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

5. Situation playbooks — things to think about
Common scenarios and the extra prompts each one demands. Use alongside the deep prompt
bank.

Domestic violence assault
• Treat pressure to the neck / hands to the throat / ‘I couldn’t breathe’ as a red flag — capture
it precisely; consider s.3A non-fatal strangulation and the elevated future-homicide risk it
signals.
• The history: previous incidents (reported or not), prior injuries, threats, coercive control,
financial control, damage to property, harm to pets.
• Children: present? Did they see or hear it? Their welfare and any Tusla referral.
• Safety planning and protective/barring/safety orders; the risk assessment; the IP’s
immediate safety after you leave.
• Reluctance and withdrawal are common — record a full first account and preserve
independent evidence (999 call, injuries, CCTV, texts) so the case does not depend solely
on the IP’s willingness later.
Night-time economy — pub, club, street
• Alcohol levels of IP and suspect; capacity to recall; fix the timeline against last orders,
CCTV clocks, taxi times.
• Glass or bottle used? Treat as a weapon — describe it, its source, the contact, and the
injuries (glassing often causes facial scarring → potential s.4).
• Door staff, bar staff, premises CCTV, street cameras — identify and preserve immediately.
• Multiple people involved — who did what; separate the IP’s attacker(s) from bystanders.
Weapon / glassing
• Full description of the weapon and how it was used; where it is now; forensic potential.
• Match injuries to the weapon; consider whether the injuries take it to s.4 (serious harm /
disfigurement).
Assault on emergency workers / retail & public-facing workers
• The IP’s role and duty at the time (Garda, ambulance, nurse, fire, transport, shop worker);
any aggravating statutory provision.
• That the IP was acting in the course of duty and how the suspect knew it.
Hate-motivated assault
• Any demonstration of hostility based on a protected characteristic — words used, symbols,
context.
• Consider the aggravated-by-hatred forms of the offence; capture the hostile words verbatim.
Group / multiple assailants
• What each person did individually, as far as the IP can say; who directed, who joined in,
who encouraged.
• Common design / joint participation; the sequence across attackers.
Mutual fight / IP may also be a suspect
• Pause on status before taking a witness statement — if the IP was a participant, consider
caution and their own liability (affray, s.2/s.3).
• Establish honestly who was the aggressor and whether either was defending themselves.
Vulnerable injured party (child, elderly, disability, mental health)
• Specialist interviewer, intermediary, supportive adult; video-recorded interview where
appropriate; pace and language suited to the person.

10

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

• Capacity to consent and to recall; corroborating evidence given any communication
difficulty.

Intoxicated, injured or unconscious IP who cannot fully recall
• Take what they can give; fill the gaps with CCTV, witnesses, medical evidence and the 999
call rather than pressing them to guess.
• Note honestly what they do not remember — do not manufacture certainty.
Historic or delayed report
• Why the delay (fear, DV, shame) — relevant and often innocent; record it.
• What evidence still exists (photos taken at the time, medical records, prior disclosures to
others).
Injuries inconsistent with the account
• Probe gently and honestly — more injuries than described, or a mechanism that doesn’t
match, needs exploring, not ignoring.
• Get the medical evidence and let it speak; do not shape the account to fit.

11

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

6. Photographs and medical evidence
• Consent and dignity. Obtain consent; photograph injuries with dignity and, where
appropriate, a same-sex member; use a scale/ruler and a body-map to record location.
• Timing and follow-up. Photograph as soon as possible and again at 24–48 hours —
bruising darkens and new marks appear. Record dates/times of each set.
• Medical statement and records. Seek a statement from the treating doctor and consent
for release of records; the medical evidence proves the harm and can lift or confirm the
charge.
• Continuity. Label and exhibit photographs, clothing and the weapon; be able to speak to
your own handling and refer to the exhibit book.
INVESTIGATOR — THINK: Will the injury still be visible for court? If not, your photographs and
the medical statement are the injury. Get both, dated, before the marks fade.

8. Model statement skeleton
A neutral spine to adapt to the case and to current Garda statement format — taken in the
injured party’s own words, first person, in sequence.
“I am [name], date of birth […], of [address]. I work as […]. I am making this statement of my
own free will.”
“I [know / do not know] the person who assaulted me. [If known: I know him/her as … for …
years; he/she lives at …]”
“On [date] at about [time] I was at [place]. [Conditions; who was with me.] Just before it
happened, …”
“[Blow by blow, in sequence: what he/she did, in what order, to what part of me, how hard,
where I was, what I was doing, what was said.]”
“As a result I suffered [each injury and its effect, body and mind]. I was treated at […]. Since
then …”
“After it, [what I did; who I told first and what I said; who called the Gardaí].”
“I did not consent to being [struck/…] and I did nothing to justify it. I am willing to attend court. I
consent to my injuries being photographed and to the release of my medical records.”
Read back, allow correction, and have the injured party adopt the statement in the standard form;
record time, date, place, who was present, and any interpreter.

14

Injured Party Statement Guide — Assault (s.2 / s.3 NFOAP 1997)

9. Common mistakes to avoid
• Letting summary language stand (“he assaulted me”) instead of the blow-by-blow
mechanism.
• Recording the visible injury but not the pain, the psychological harm, or a brief loss of
consciousness — losing the s.3 ‘harm’.
• Missing the neck/breathing detail that points to s.3A strangulation, especially in a domestic.
• Leaving self-defence and consent unaddressed, so the defence fills the gap.
• Relying on a later or dock identification instead of a recorded first description and the
viewing conditions.
• Not securing perishable evidence — CCTV, clothing, follow-up injury photographs — the
same day.
• Leading the witness or shaping the account to fit the injuries; take it in their words and let
the medical evidence speak.

End of guide

Chapter 3 — Threats, Harassment, Coercion, and False
Imprisonment
This chapter covers the offences that do not require contact but interfere seriously with the person’s liberty,
psychological safety, or peace of mind. Operationally they are frequently chained: threats accompany
harassment, which escalates to coercion, which culminates in false imprisonment or assault. They form a
continuum that the investigating Garda must read as such.

3.1 Threats to Kill or Cause Serious Harm — s.5 NFOAP 1997
THREATS TO KILL / SERIOUS HARM: A person who, without lawful excuse, makes to another a
threat, by any means intending the other to believe it will be carried out, to kill or cause serious harm to
that other or a third person. Contrary to s.5 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 10 years on indictment.

Elements
91. Threat by any means — verbal, written, electronic, signed, gestured, conveyed via intermediary.
92. To kill or cause serious harm — to IP or to a third person.
93. Intent that the IP believe it will be carried out — not necessarily intent actually to carry it out, but intent
that the IP take it seriously.

Operational Context
S.5 charges are common in DV, drug-debt intimidation, gang feuds, and protective relationship breakdowns.
Communications evidence is central. The s.5 charge often runs in parallel with criminal-damage threats (s.3
CDA 1991), harassment (s.10 NFOAP 1997), and coercion (s.9 NFOAP 1997).

First Actions on Report
94. Sit IP down. Calm account. Verbatim of the threat.
95. Capture communications immediately — screenshots with metadata, recording of voicemails (transfer to
evidential storage), preservation of message threads.
96. Witness identification — anyone present when threat made, anyone IP told.
97. Safety planning — ongoing risk; consider safety plan, address-marker on PULSE, protection order route.
98. PULSE — Threats to Kill / Serious Harm; cross-link to any prior incidents.

Evidence Targets
• Communications — every message, call log, voicemail, social-media exchange. Metadata, account
verification.
• Witness statements on the threat being made / heard.
• Prior history — PULSE entries showing escalation pattern.
• Means evidence — does suspect have access to weapons, history of violence?

Suspect Strategy
S.5 is arrestable (max 10 yrs). S.4 CJA 1984. Search of address under s.10 CJA 2006 may be productive for
devices, written material, weapons.

Interview Plan
99. Identification — do they admit making the communications.
100. Words used — admit / deny.
101. Intent — did they intend the IP to believe the threat.
102. Context — relationship with IP, prior incidents.

103. Means — access to weapons, history.

work. Many IPs report later that this conversation was what gave them confidence to follow through.

3.3 Coercion — s.9 NFOAP 1997
COERCION: Using violence to or intimidation of another person or a member of his or her family, or
injuring or damaging his or her property, or persistently following him or her about, or watching or
besetting his or her residence or workplace, or following him or her in a disorderly manner in or through
any street, with a view to compelling that person to abstain from doing or to do an act which he or she
has a legal right to do or to abstain from doing. Contrary to s.9 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 5 years.

Operational Context

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Coercion is the offence of forcing someone to act, or not act, by intimidatory means. Examples: forcing a
witness to recant; forcing a debtor to pay; forcing an IP not to engage with Gardaí; forcing a tenant to leave;
forcing a worker not to participate in lawful industrial activity.
Inner-city Dublin presentations: drug-debt coercion (“pay or we burn your mother’s house”), feud coercion
(witness intimidation), DV coercion (force return to relationship), commercial coercion (eviction-style).

Elements
128. Violence / intimidation / property damage / following / watching / besetting / disorderly following.
129. Of the IP or family member.
130. With a view to compelling — the suspect’s purpose.
131. Abstain from / do an act — the IP being targeted to alter conduct.
132. Legal right — the IP’s right to do or not do is protected; coercion to do something unlawful is a different
offence.

Pattern evidence — multiple incidents.
Safety planning — immediate.

3.4 Stalking — s.39 CJ(Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023
STALKING: A person who, by an act or acts, intentionally or recklessly causes another to fear that

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

violence will be used against him or her or a third person, or to suffer serious alarm or distress that has
a substantial adverse impact on his or her usual day-to-day activities, is guilty of stalking. Contrary to
s.39 CJ(Misc.Prov.)A 2023. Maximum: 10 years.
New offence introduced 2023. Captures the persistent, fear-causing conduct that formerly was charged under
s.10 NFOAP harassment. Specific examples include physical following, surveillance, repeated contact, leaving
items, gift-giving in unwanted contexts, online tracking, identity manipulation.

Investigation
As harassment, with additional focus on:
• The IP’s day-to-day impact — work attendance, sleep, social activities, mental health, change of address.
• The acts as constituting a pattern of stalking specifically (versus general harassment).
• Online and surveillance dimension — platforms, devices, location data, account access.

Charge Selection
Charge under s.39 where the elements are met. Alternative s.10 NFOAP 1997. Parallel s.5 (threats), s.9
(coercion), DV Act 2018 (coercive control) as facts support.

Defence Will Attack
142. Mens rea — “My client did not intend or recklessly cause fear; the IP misinterpreted innocent conduct.”
143. Substantial adverse impact — was the impact actually substantial?
144. Acts — each act assessed; lawful conduct (e.g. driving past a former joint address) may be argued as
innocent.
How to Bullet-Proof
• Impact evidence — documented IP changes; medical evidence if treated; witness corroboration of
observable changes.
• Acts evidenced individually and together; pattern.
• Communications and surveillance trail.

See: Harassment (3.2); Threats (3.1); Coercion (3.3); DV (Vol 4); Case Law Appendix.

3.6 Child Abduction — s.16 NFOAP 1997
CHILD ABDUCTION: A person who unlawfully takes or detains a child under the age of 16 with intent
to remove the child from the lawful control of the parent / guardian / person having lawful control.
Contrary to s.16 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 7 years.
Operationally rare but high-priority. Most child abductions in Ireland are intra-familial (parental child abduction
in custody disputes); stranger abduction is rare. Either way, the response is rapid and Detective-led, with Tusla,
mental health services, and (in international cases) DPP Central Authority for Hague Convention engagement.

Operational Notes
• Amber Alert protocol — not used in Ireland in formal sense but rapid public-information response via
Garda Press Office.
• Border / port watch — international abduction; engage Department of Justice and embassy where required.
• Tusla involvement — child welfare assessment immediately.
• Civil remedies in parallel — court orders for return.
• Family liaison — with abducting parent’s family also, where possible, for intelligence and influence.

Cross-References
See: False Imprisonment (3.5); DV / coercive control (Vol 4); Case Law Appendix; international protocols (Vol
9).

This chapter covers offences that do not require contact-based assault but engage the person’s physical integrity
through risk creation, substance exposure, or specifically directed against persons in protected functions
(Gardaí, emergency workers).

4.1 Endangerment — s.13 NFOAP 1997
ENDANGERMENT: A person who intentionally or recklessly engages in conduct which creates a
substantial risk of death or serious harm to another. Contrary to s.13 NFOAP 1997. Maximum: 7 years.

Operational Context
Wide-spectrum offence. Examples: throwing a bottle from a balcony into a crowd; discharging a firearm in a
public area; driving at a crowd; dousing premises in petrol without ignition; arming a weapon and pointing it
at a person; dangerous group activities (rooftop chases, jumping between buildings) where others put at risk.
S.13 is a useful charge where the conduct created serious risk but did not result in actual injury, or where the
actual injury under-charges the risk created.

Elements
172.
173.
174.
175.

Conduct — act or omission.
Intentional or reckless.
Creates a substantial risk.
Of death or serious harm to another.

Key Authority
DPP v Cagney and McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111 — Recklessness
Already cited. Subjective awareness of the risk required. Critical for s.13 because the offence is constructively
defined by the risk created — the accused’s awareness of that risk is the live issue.

Investigation
176. Establish the conduct — witness statements, CCTV, forensic evidence.
177. Establish the substantial risk — the act itself often speaks (e.g. firing a weapon), but sometimes requires
expert evidence (e.g. ballistic analysis of an aimed shot).
178. Establish another at risk — identify by name where possible; statement of person at risk.
179. Mens rea — the accused’s state of mind probed in interview.

Appendix — Defences, Burdens of Proof, Charge
Selection, File Craft & Trial Practice
This appendix is the operational legal-end resource for Vol 2 offences. It addresses what a probationer needs
to understand for the first time and what an experienced member needs at the borderline cases. Sections move
from the burden-of-proof framework, through each defence as it operates in court, to charge selection at the file
build stage, to file-craft for serious crime, and finally to trial-end practice.

Part A — Burdens of Proof in Person-Crime
A.1 The General Framework
Irish criminal law operates on a structured allocation of burdens. Three categories matter:
• Legal burden on prosecution: prove every element of the offence beyond reasonable doubt. This includes
actus reus and mens rea.
• Evidential burden on accused: where a defence is raised, the accused must adduce evidence sufficient to
put the defence in play. This is a low threshold — a credible foundation in the evidence is enough.
• Legal burden on prosecution to disprove the defence: once put in play, the prosecution must disprove the
defence beyond reasonable doubt. This is the critical step that flips many defendants’ cases into reasonable
doubt.

A.2 Exception: Insanity (Reversed Legal Burden)
The defence of insanity under s.5 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006 is a true exception. Where the accused
raises insanity, the burden of proof is on the accused to prove insanity on the balance of probabilities. This is
the only legal burden squarely on the defence in standard person-crime cases. The same applies in diminished
responsibility under s.6 of the 2006 Act — the accused bears the legal burden of proof on the balance of
probabilities.

A.3 The Inferential Picture
Beyond the formal burdens, several evidential inferences operate in practice:
• S.4(2) CJA 1964 — the accused is presumed to have intended the natural and probable consequences of
his conduct (rebuttable).
• S.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984 (as amended) — adverse inferences from silence on specified matters,
prerequisite-conditional.
• DPP v Hull — intent may be inferred from the nature of the act, weapon, target, force, words, conduct.

A.4 Practical Implications for the Investigator
Understanding burdens tells the investigator what evidence to produce:
• Build the prosecution case on every element, including mens rea.
• Anticipate every defence likely to be raised — and produce evidence that will allow the prosecution to
disprove it.
• Preserve mental-health material in any case where insanity / diminished responsibility might enter
(homicide especially).

Part B — The Defences in Person-Crime
B.1 Self-Defence (Common Law / s.18 NFOAP / CL(D&D)A 2011)
Operative Test
Hybrid: honest belief in need to use force (subjective) + reasonable force in the circumstances as the accused
subjectively believed them (objective measure against subjective view of circumstances). In the dwelling, s.2

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CL(D&D)A 2011 governs and there is no duty to retreat.
How it Plays at Trial
The accused’s account in interview is the foundation. Defence will lead with the perceived threat — the IP’s
words, weapon, conduct, prior history. Defence will frame the force used as reasonable response. Prosecution
rebuts by showing: (a) no honest belief in threat (manufactured / pretextual); or (b) the force used was
disproportionate — multiple blows after the threat ended, weapon used where bare hands would suffice, blows
to vulnerable areas, persistence after IP incapacitated.
Excessive Self-Defence (Dwyer)
If the first limb is met (honest belief in need to use force) but the second is not (force was excessive), the partial
defence reduces murder to manslaughter. This is a uniquely Irish position and a meaningful trial outcome in
marginal cases.
Practice Point for Investigator
• Document the threat picture as the accused described it — do not edit out details that may help the accused.
• Pathologist analysis of force used — number of blows, location, severity — is the prosecution’s rebuttal
evidence.
• Scene reconstruction — distances, lighting, positions — supports or undermines the accused’s account.

B.2 Provocation (MacEoin / Davis / Heffernan)
Operative Test
Subjective. The accused must have lost self-control as a result of the provocation; the loss must have been
sudden and temporary; mere anger or upset is insufficient (Davis behavioural threshold). Heffernan opens the
door to cumulative / sustained-provocation analysis.
How it Plays at Trial
Partial defence to murder only — reduces to manslaughter. Defence leads with the provoking conduct of the
deceased; the accused’s account of loss of self-control; the immediacy of the response. Prosecution rebuts by
showing: (a) the response was planned (acquisition of weapon, lying in wait); (b) cooling-off opportunity
existed between provocation and response; (c) the accused was in control — evidenced by purposeful conduct,
multiple deliberate acts, statements of intent.
Practice Point for Investigator
• Document the timeline with precision — minute-by-minute where possible.
• Document any pre-act conduct showing planning (texts, calls, weapon acquisition, route choice).
• Document any post-act conduct showing control (calm calls, lies, cover-up).
• Anticipate sustained-provocation cases especially in DV — build the history of provocation across the
relationship.

B.3 Diminished Responsibility (s.6 Insanity Act 2006)
Operative Test
(a) Mental disorder at the time of the killing; (b) the disorder substantially diminished responsibility for the act;
(c) the disorder is not such as to entitle the accused to the special verdict of insanity. Legal burden on accused
on balance of probabilities.
How it Plays at Trial
Partial defence to murder only — reduces to manslaughter. The defence will lead psychiatric evidence (typically
a forensic psychiatrist with a clinical examination of the accused and review of medical history). Prosecution
may instruct its own forensic psychiatrist. The trial becomes a forensic psychiatry contest at the expert-evidence
stage.
Practice Point for Investigator
• Preserve mental-health history early — GP records, hospital admissions, prescriptions, previous diagnoses.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Document any pre-incident indicators of mental state — family observations, work records, recent
behavioural changes.
• Consider commissioning Garda-side forensic psychiatric examination through DPP.
• Custody-stage mental health assessment if any indicator at presentation — do not miss.

B.4 Insanity (s.5 Insanity Act 2006)
Operative Test
Mental disorder at the time of the act such that the accused (a) did not know the nature and quality of the act,
OR (b) did not know it was wrong, OR (c) was unable to refrain from committing the act. Legal burden on
accused on balance of probabilities.
Outcome
Special verdict — not guilty by reason of insanity. The accused is detained under mental-health framework
rather than imprisonment; the Mental Health (Criminal Law) Review Board considers detention status
periodically.
Practice Point
Same as diminished responsibility — preserve mental-health material early.

B.5 Consent (Assault Only)
Operative Test
Consent is a defence to s.2 NFOAP. Consent is not a defence to assault causing harm (s.3) or above outside
recognised exceptions (R v Brown, persuasive; Irish position aligns). The recognised exceptions include
organised sport, reasonable surgical intervention, body modification within accepted limits, certain religious
practices.
How it Plays at Trial
Defence rarely succeeds outside the recognised contexts. In DV cases where the suspect claims consensual
roughness, the defence faces R v Brown headwinds; the prosecution case stands on the basic absence of
meaningful consent and the harm threshold.
Practice Point
• Investigate the relationship context but do not accept consent claims as defeating s.3 / s.4 cases.
• IP’s account of consent or non-consent is central but the legal threshold is the harm threshold.

B.6 Defence of Property
Operative Test
S.18 NFOAP 1997 permits force in defence of property if reasonable in the circumstances as the user believed
them. The threshold for reasonable force in defence of property is lower than for defence of person — deadly
force is rarely reasonable to protect property alone.
Practice Point
Where the accused claims defence of property in a violent altercation, scrutinise whether the property was
actually under threat and whether the force used was a reasonable response. Excess force in defence of property
is unlikely to qualify even for partial defence in homicide cases — the lawful-purpose ground is narrower.

B.7 Defence of Others
Operative Test
S.18(1)(a) NFOAP — “himself / herself or another from injury, assault, or detention” — extends the selfdefence framework to defence of third parties. Same test as personal self-defence: honest belief + reasonable
force on subjectively believed circumstances.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

B.8 Duress
Operative Test
Defence of duress available where the accused acted under threats of imminent death or serious harm to self or
others; threats from a third party; no reasonable opportunity to escape; reasonable response in the
circumstances. Duress is NOT a defence to murder.
Practice Point
Duress is occasionally raised in joint-enterprise murder cases (“I was forced to participate”). The Irish position
is that duress does not defeat murder. In secondary-party murder via joint enterprise, duress can affect the
foresight analysis but is not itself a complete answer.

B.9 Necessity
Operative Test
Necessity (sometimes called the defence of necessity / lesser-of-two-evils) is recognised in narrow
circumstances in Irish criminal law. The accused acted to prevent a greater evil, the response was proportionate,
and there was no reasonable alternative. Necessity is NOT a defence to murder (subject to limited debate).

B.10 Concurrent Defences
It is common in homicide trials for multiple defences to be raised together: self-defence (full or excessive),
provocation, diminished responsibility. The defences are not mutually exclusive at the pleading stage — they
are presented in the alternative. The investigator should anticipate all live possibilities and build evidence that
addresses each.

C.3 s.3 versus s.2 NFOAP — The Harm Threshold
Investigators face this borderline most often. The key is the definition of “harm” in s.1: harm to body or mind,
including pain and unconsciousness. The IP’s account, the medical evidence (even informal medical attention),
the photographs of injuries, all build the case for s.3. Where pain alone is established credibly, s.3 is supported.
Where no harm beyond contact is evidenced, s.2 is the charge.

C.5 False Imprisonment — Recognise the Offence
S.15 NFOAP carries life imprisonment but is routinely under-recognised at investigation stage. Recognise it

• DV detention scenarios — keeping IP in the home, taking keys, blocking exits.
• Group restraint at scenes — gang-style holding.
• Coercive-control cases — sustained restriction of movement may engage s.15 alongside s.9 coercion and
DV Act 2018 s.39 coercive control.

C.6 Coercive Control — s.39 Domestic Violence Act 2018
Distinct offence from harassment / stalking. The conduct must be (a) persistent, (b) in the context of a
relationship as defined, (c) such that it would cause the IP fear that violence will be used or have a serious
impact on day-to-day activities. The Irish offence is structured around the relationship context and the
cumulative effect on the IP. Charge under s.39 DV Act 2018 in intimate-partner / domestic contexts where the
pattern fits.
