D.3 Procedural Completeness Checklist for Serious Files
• Caution at arrest, repeated at custody, repeated at interview commencement.
• Solicitor access — Gormley consultation pre-interview vindicated and documented.
• Solicitor presence in interview — facilitated if requested (post-Doyle position).
• Inference warnings (s.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984) — only where prerequisites met, in prescribed form (Buck).
• Audio / video recording of interviews — mandatory for serious offences; recording integrity.
• Custody record — contemporaneous, complete, member-in-charge entries.
• Medical assessment — where suspect indicators, IP examination, or general welfare requires.
• Searches — independent warrant authority (Damache) unless statutory power on arrest.
• Forensic chain of custody — unbroken, documented.
• Scene preservation — documented from first contact to handover.

D.4 Antecedent Statement and Victim Impact Statement
At sentencing stage, two key pieces of file-craft:
Antecedent Statement
Garda-prepared summary of the accused for the court — prior convictions, custodial history, antecedents. Must
be accurate, fair, and based on documentation. Errors undermine the prosecution credibility at the most critical
procedural moment.
Victim Impact Statement
Statutory right of the IP under the Criminal Justice (Victims of Crime) Act 2017. The investigating Garda
assists the IP in preparing the statement where appropriate. The statement is read by the IP at sentencing or,
where impractical, presented in writing. It should address physical, psychological, financial, and social impact.

Part E — Trial Practice (Garda Perspective)
E.1 Book of Evidence (Indictable Trials)
The Book of Evidence is the formal documentary set served on the accused in indictable proceedings. The
Garda investigation file feeds the Book through DPP review and editing. Quality of investigation directly
determines Book quality. Witness statements must be: in the witness’s own words; signed; dated; declared (s.21
CJA 1984 declaration).

E.2 Pre-Trial Disclosure
Beyond the Book, the prosecution must disclose all material that may assist the defence or undermine the

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

prosecution. Garda investigation generates this material: every statement (used or unused), every memo, every
CCTV review, every digital extract. Material that supports the defence (favourable to accused) must be
disclosed. The investigator builds the disclosure register from day one.

E.3 Section 21 CJA 1984 Statement — Statutory Declaration
Witnesses’ written statements for indictable proceedings must be declared under s.21 — the witness signs a
declaration that the statement is true to the best of their knowledge and belief, and that they understand they are
liable to prosecution for any wilfully false statement. The declaration form is the standard Garda witness
statement template.

E.4 Cross-Examination Anticipation
Every Garda witness statement, notebook, and memo will be cross-examined. Pre-trial preparation:
• Read the file end-to-end shortly before trial.
• Reconcile notebook entries against statement, memo, custody record, exhibit list.
• Anticipate the defence theory — what is the defence going to put?
• Anticipate the procedural attacks — lawful arrest, caution, custody, solicitor, inference warning.
• Have the case law touchstones in mind — Walsh on arrest, Gormley on solicitor, Damache on warrant, JC
on exclusion, Buck on inferences.

E.5 Court Demeanour
Person-crime trials are emotionally charged. The Garda witness:
• Address answers to the court, not the questioner.
• Stay within own knowledge — do not speculate.
• Acknowledge limitations honestly — “I cannot recall” if accurate; refer to notebook with permission.
• Resist hostile cross-examination by remaining factual.
• Do not editorialise about the accused or the IP.

E.6 Plea Negotiation
Many person-crime cases resolve by plea before trial. The Garda role is supportive:
• DPP and counsel lead the negotiation.
• Investigator’s view on charge appropriateness may be sought.
• Where a plea to a lesser charge is contemplated (e.g., murder to manslaughter on provocation), the
investigator briefs the IP’s family / Family Liaison Officer.
• Antecedent statement and Victim Impact Statement prepared in advance of plea hearing.

E.7 Sentencing Hearing
At sentencing the Garda may be called to outline the facts (where the trial court did not hear evidence — e.g.,
guilty plea). The factual outline must be: accurate, balanced, not sensationalised. The court will hear:
• Garda outline of facts.
• Garda evidence on antecedents.
• Victim Impact Statement.
• Defence mitigation — personal circumstances, remorse, rehabilitation, prior good character, plea /
cooperation.
• Sentencing judge applies Tiernan proportionality framework.

Part F — Cross-Cutting Points for Experienced Members
F.1 The Borderline Murder File

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Where the elements of murder are debatable — contested intent, possible provocation, possible self-defence —
the investigator’s job is not to predetermine the outcome but to build the file so that the DPP has the material
to make the charge decision and the trial court the material to make the verdict decision.
In practice, this means: charge murder on the strength of the prima facie evidence; document all defence
indicators thoroughly; do not over-claim or under-claim. The file should be one a prosecution counsel can argue
and a defence counsel can engage with fully.

F.2 The DV-Context Killing
Sustained-provocation cases (Heffernan) and coercive-control history may legitimately reduce a murder to
manslaughter at trial or on plea. The investigator should not treat the historical pattern of abuse against the
killer as a side issue — it is central to the case. Build the history. Document it. The DPP will work with that
material.

F.3 Multi-Suspect Cases (Joint Enterprise)
Each suspect must be considered individually for foresight (Reilly). Generic group analysis is appellate
vulnerability. The investigator files separately on each accused: their planning involvement, their role at scene,
their post-incident conduct, their statements / silence. The file conveys an individualised case for jointenterprise liability against each.

F.4 Custody-Derived Evidence in Modern Practice
Post-Gormley / Doyle the operational tempo has shifted. Pre-interview consultation is the norm, not the
exception. Solicitor presence in interview is facilitated where requested. The investigator who understands this
works with the regime rather than against it — voluntary admissions are made under the regime, and they are
robust at trial because the regime is observed.

F.5 Inference Warnings — Strict Compliance
Buck enforces the inference prerequisites strictly. Read the prescribed warning in full; pause; record the
suspect’s response; allow consultation if the suspect indicates they want it. Inference points won at the interview
stage become inference points lost at trial if any prerequisite is skipped.

F.6 Working with Forensic Disciplines
Person-crime investigation now routinely involves:
• Pathology — cause of death, mechanism, time of death indicators, injury patterns.
• Forensic biology — blood, semen, saliva, DNA recovery and comparison.
• Forensic toxicology — substance presence, intoxication, drug-facilitated offences.
• Digital forensics — phones, computers, vehicles, CCTV, communications data.
• Ballistics where firearms involved.
• Forensic psychology / psychiatry where mental state is in issue.
The experienced member coordinates these disciplines proactively. Forensic Science Ireland (FSI) interface;
State Pathologist’s office; Garda Cyber Crime Bureau; Garda Forensic Science Liaison.

F.7 Family Liaison — Homicide and Serious Injury
FLO assignment is structured by the Garda Síochána family liaison guidance. The FLO maintains continuous
contact, provides updates within operational confines, attends court with the family, and is a single trusted
point. The role is operationally separate from the investigation team to preserve the family’s ability to engage.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix — Evidence and Admissibility (Vol 2 —
Person)
This appendix addresses the law of evidence as it operates in person-crime investigation and trial. The areas
covered: hearsay; opinion (including expert); character (good and bad, prior convictions); similar fact /
propensity; prior consistent and inconsistent statements; corroboration; recent complaint; documentary and
electronic evidence. These rules determine what evidence the investigator can rely on at trial, how that evidence
must be presented, and where the procedural traps lie.

Part A — Hearsay
A.1 The Rule
Hearsay is an out-of-court statement adduced to prove the truth of its contents. The general rule in Irish criminal
law is that hearsay is inadmissible. Subject to statutory and common-law exceptions.

A.2 What is and is not Hearsay
• A statement repeated by a witness, intended to prove the truth of what the original speaker said — hearsay.
• A statement repeated to prove that it was said (not its truth) — NOT hearsay (e.g., showing notice, state of
mind).
• The witness’s own previous out-of-court statement, repeated by another — hearsay (subject to limited
exceptions).
• Implied assertions — conduct from which an assertion is inferred — can be hearsay in some circumstances.

A.3 Common-Law Exceptions
• Confessions / admissions by the accused — admissible against the accused (subject to voluntariness,
Madden).
• Dying declarations — in homicide, statements by the deceased shortly before death where the deceased
had no hope of recovery, as to the cause of death.
• Res gestae — spontaneous declarations made during or immediately after a startling event.
• Statements of deceased persons — limited exceptions for statements against interest, in the course of duty,
etc.
• Public documents — official records admissible as evidence of their contents under appropriate conditions.

A.4 Statutory Exceptions
Criminal Evidence Act 1992 — Documentary / Business Records
Permits documentary evidence in business records and statements made in the course of business / occupation
where the maker is unavailable. Important practical exception — covers many investigative documentary
records (bank, telephone, business).
Criminal Justice Act 2006 — Out-of-Court Statements (s.16)
Where a witness is intimidated, in fear, has died, or is otherwise unavailable, the witness’s prior out-of-court
statement may be admitted with court permission, subject to safeguards. This is the principal modern statutory
inroad on the hearsay rule in serious crime.
Operational Application
• Where a witness becomes intimidated or fearful, preserve the prior statement carefully.
• Application to admit out-of-court statement is made via DPP / counsel at trial.
• Court considers fairness, reliability, the accused’s ability to challenge.
• Particularly important in gang-related and DV intimidation cases.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Children’s Evidence — Criminal Evidence Act 1992
Video-recorded interviews of children (and certain vulnerable adult witnesses) may be admitted as evidencein-chief, subject to procedural conditions and the witness being available for cross-examination.

A.5 Practical Investigation Points
• Recognise the difference between what a witness saw (admissible direct evidence) and what a witness was
told (hearsay).
• Identify and approach original sources — the person who made the statement, not the person who heard it.
• Preserve documentary records under the Criminal Evidence Act 1992 framework.
• In intimidation cases, build the file so that s.16 CJA 2006 application is supportable — timeline of
intimidation, witness availability efforts.

Part B — Opinion and Expert Evidence
B.1 The General Rule
Witnesses give evidence of facts, not opinion. Opinion evidence is inadmissible, with two main exceptions:
expert opinion within the expert’s field, and lay opinion as to matters of common observation (e.g., approximate
speed, intoxication appearance, age).

B.2 Expert Evidence — The Framework
Expert evidence is admissible where:
213.

B.3 Common Experts in Person Crime
• Pathologist (cause of death, mechanism, timing, injury interpretation).
• Forensic biologist (DNA, blood pattern analysis, body fluid identification).
• Forensic toxicologist (substance presence, levels, effects).
• Forensic psychiatrist (mental state at material time, diagnosis, capacity).
• Forensic psychologist (psychological state, vulnerability, memory).
• Digital forensic examiner (device extraction, communications analysis, location).
• Ballistic / weapons expert (firearm identification, ammunition, projectile path).
• Forensic odontologist (bite mark identification, dental ID).

B.4 Investigator Engagement with Experts
• Engage early — preliminary discussion before formal instruction.
• Provide complete material — incomplete instructions yield limited opinions.
• Understand limits — an expert’s opinion is only as good as the facts and methodology supporting it.
• Anticipate defence expert challenge — the prosecution expert will be cross-examined; the defence may
instruct an alternative expert.

B.5 Lay Opinion
Within narrow limits, lay witnesses can give opinion on matters of ordinary observation:
• Speed of a vehicle (rough estimate).
• Appearance of intoxication.
• Age (approximate).
• Identity of person known to the witness.
• Emotional state observed (“she appeared upset”).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part C — Character Evidence
C.1 Good Character
The accused may put forward evidence of good character. This is typically done through character witnesses
or through cross-examination of prosecution witnesses. The legal effect is twofold:
• Credibility — the accused’s denial / account is more credible because of good character.
• Propensity — the accused is less likely to have committed the offence.
Where the accused puts forward good character, the prosecution may rebut with evidence of bad character (with
some limitations).

C.2 Bad Character of the Accused — The General Exclusion
As a general rule, the prosecution cannot adduce evidence of the accused’s bad character or prior convictions
in chief. The accused’s previous bad conduct is irrelevant to whether they committed the offence charged. The
reasoning: the jury may convict on the basis of past conduct rather than the evidence in the present case.

C.3 Exceptions — When Bad Character Becomes Admissible
Putting Character in Issue
If the accused puts their own character in issue (by leading good character evidence, or attacking the character
of prosecution witnesses), the door is opened to rebuttal evidence of bad character.
Similar Fact / Propensity Evidence
Where the prior conduct is so closely similar to the present offence that the similarity is itself probative — not
of propensity but of pattern or identity — the evidence may be admissible. The test (drawing on R v Boardman
and Irish case law) is that the probative value must substantially outweigh the prejudicial effect.
Examples
• Multiple complainants of the same accused with closely similar accounts — cross-admissible where
similarity is striking.
• A signature method of offending — weapons, words, ritual.
• Where identity is the live issue and the prior offence supplies evidence of identity (similar modus operandi).
Background Evidence
Evidence of the accused’s prior conduct may be admissible where it forms an essential part of the background
to the offence — the context without which the prosecution case cannot fairly be presented. Examples: prior
DV in a DV homicide; prior threats by the accused against the IP; the relationship history.
Cross-Examination on Bad Character
If the accused gives evidence, they may be cross-examined on their character only in defined circumstances
under the relevant statutory regime — typically where the accused has attacked another’s character, put their
own character in issue, or where the evidence is otherwise admissible.

C.4 Practical Investigation Implications
• PULSE history is relevant for the investigation’s tactical decisions but is not generally admissible at trial.
• Where prior similar offences exist, build the file with awareness that a similar-fact application may be
made.
• Document the background context of the present offence rigorously — the relationship history may become
admissible as background evidence.

Part D — Prior Statements of Witnesses
D.1 Prior Inconsistent Statements

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

A witness whose evidence at trial differs materially from a prior statement may be cross-examined on the
inconsistency. The procedural mechanism:
214.

D.2 Prior Consistent Statements — The Rule Against
Generally, a witness’s prior consistent statements are inadmissible — they do not strengthen credibility merely
by repetition. Exceptions:
• Rebuttal of recent fabrication — where the defence suggests the witness has recently invented their account,
prior consistent statements predating the alleged fabrication may be admitted to rebut.
• Recent complaint in sexual offence cases (now significantly broadened by statute — see Volume 6 for
sexual offences treatment).
• Statements admissible as part of the res gestae.
• Statements admissible as part of the narrative.

D.3 Operational Implication
The witness’s contemporaneous account (statement, memo, 999 call recording) is preserved not merely for the
investigation but for trial use — to rebut allegations of recent fabrication, to refresh memory, to test consistency.

Part E — Corroboration
E.1 The Modern Position
Corroboration is independent evidence implicating the accused in a material respect. The historical mandatorycorroboration warnings (for accomplice evidence, certain sexual offence evidence, etc.) have been largely
abolished or made discretionary by statute. The court retains discretion to warn the jury about evidence
requiring caution.

E.2 Where Corroboration Matters
• Identification — Casey warning structures the jury’s approach; corroboration evidence (other witnesses,
CCTV, forensic) strengthens the prosecution case.
• Accomplice evidence — historically subject to mandatory warning; now discretionary. The credibility of
the accomplice as a self-interested witness is for the jury but corroboration helps.
• Confession-only cases — a case resting solely on disputed confession is vulnerable; corroboration
(forensic, witness, opportunity, motive) strengthens substantially.

E.3 Operational Implication
• Even where corroboration is not legally required, the investigation should seek corroborating evidence
wherever possible.
• Forensic and digital evidence often provides independent corroboration of witness accounts.

Part F — Documentary and Electronic Evidence
F.1 Documents — Authenticity and Best Evidence
Documentary evidence must be authenticated — someone establishes what it is, where it came from, and that
it is what it purports to be. The best-evidence rule (now significantly modernised) historically required the
original; copies are routinely admissible today subject to authenticity.

F.2 Electronic Evidence — The Modern Reality
Most person-crime investigations now turn on electronic evidence at some point: CCTV, mobile phone data,
social media, communications data, ANPR, body-worn camera. The framework:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Lawful acquisition — statutory authority, warrant, consent, or arrest-incidental seizure.
• Chain of custody — unbroken documented record from seizure to court.
• Integrity — hash values, write-protected imaging, verified extraction.
• Examination report — forensic examiner statement describing methodology and findings.
• Original device preserved — for defence examination if challenged.

F.3 Communications Data Access
Access to communications data (call records, cell-site, internet logs) is regulated by the Communications
(Retention of Data) Acts 2011, 2022, structured by ECJ Tele2 / Watson and subsequent jurisprudence. Access
requires:
• Statutory authority for the requesting officer.
• Necessity in connection with serious crime.
• Prior independent review (judicial or designated authority).
• Documented purpose.

F.4 CCTV and Video Evidence
• Capture parameters — location of camera, angle, lighting, resolution, frame rate, timezone.
• Continuity — documented chain from camera through download to court production.
• Integrity — original copy preserved; working copies for analysis.
• Statement from the person who downloaded / produced the footage.

Part G — Interview Evidence
G.1 Admissibility — Madden Voluntariness
Confessions and admissions are admissible only if voluntary. The prosecution proves voluntariness beyond
reasonable doubt. Voluntariness is undermined by oppression, threat, inducement, denial of solicitor access
(Healy / Gormley), denial of basic dignity, failure of medical assessment where needed.

G.2 Recording Requirements
Audio and video recording of interviews is mandatory for serious offences. Failure to record raises admissibility
concerns. Recording integrity is itself an evidential matter — the original is preserved; working copies for
review; statement from custody team on recording process.

G.4 Right to Silence — Beyond Inferences

Part H — Specific Evidentiary Rules in Person Crime
H.1 Identification — Casey Warning and Procedure
Where identification is the substantial basis of the prosecution case, the Casey warning is mandatory at trial.
The investigation supports the warning by structured procedure:
• VIPER (Video Identification Parade Electronic Recording) where formal identification needed.
• Documented original sighting conditions (lighting, distance, duration, obstruction).
• ADVOKATE assessment of the witness sighting.
• Avoidance of cross-contamination between witnesses.
• Photographs and video showing scene from witness vantage point.

H.2 Recent Complaint in Person Crime (Beyond Sexual Offences)
The rules on recent complaint are most developed in sexual offences (treated in Volume 6). In other person
crime, prior complaint by an IP may be admissible as part of the narrative, to rebut recent fabrication, or as part
of the res gestae. The contemporaneous complaint preserves credibility.

H.3 Medical Evidence of Injury
Medical evidence of injury supports the harm / serious harm threshold:
• Hospital records of attendance, examination, treatment.
• Treating doctor / nurse statement — within their treating role.
• Expert evidence where injury significance is at issue — specialist report (e.g., neurologist for head injury
sequelae).
• Photographs of injuries at multiple stages (immediate, evolving, healing).

H.4 The IP’s State at the Time — Vulnerability Evidence
Where the IP’s vulnerability is relevant (sentencing aggravation, motive analysis, susceptibility to coercion),
evidence of the IP’s state is admissible:
• Age, disability, illness.
• Relationship of trust / dependence on the accused.
• Witness evidence of IP’s functioning at the relevant time.
• Medical evidence where appropriate.

Part I — Disclosure
I.1 The Prosecution Duty
The prosecution must disclose all material that may assist the defence or undermine the prosecution case. This
includes:
• Witness statements not in the Book of Evidence.
• Memos, notebook entries, custody records relating to the investigation.
• Forensic working papers, examiner notes.
• CCTV reviewed and not used.
• Communications data accessed.
• Material favourable to the accused — alternative suspect leads, exculpatory witness accounts.

I.2 The Investigator’s Role

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Disclosure starts at investigation. The investigator:
• Maintains a disclosure register from day one of the investigation — every piece of material generated or
considered.
• Identifies material that may be favourable to the accused and flags it for DPP / counsel.
• Does not edit material to suppress unhelpful elements — the duty runs to the prosecution case as a whole.
• Treats post-charge enquiries with continued disclosure rigour — the duty is ongoing.

Appendix — Statutory Anchors (Vol 2 — Person)
This appendix gathers the precise statutory wording for every principal Vol 2 offence and every key procedural
/ sentencing statute that engages person-crime investigation. Sections are grouped by area: homicide, assault,
threats / liberty offences, endangerment, assault on Garda, custody and detention, sentencing, victims, and
adjacent legislation.
Verification reminder: statutes are amended. Verify the live consolidated text on revisedacts.lawreform.ie or
irishstatutebook.ie before relying on wording in evidence.

Part A — Homicide
A.1 Murder — s.4 Criminal Justice Act 1964
S.4 partially codifies the mens rea of murder — the actus reus and substantive offence remain common law.
s.4(1): Where a person kills another unlawfully the killing shall not be murder unless the accused person
intended to kill, or cause serious injury to, some person, whether the person actually killed or not.
s.4(2): The accused person shall be presumed to have intended the natural and probable consequences of his
conduct; but this presumption may be rebutted.
Operational Application
• S.4(1) sets the mens rea: intent to kill OR intent to cause serious injury. The intent need not be directed at
the actual victim — a transferred-intent case (intended to kill A, killed B) is still murder.
• S.4(2) is the inference provision: the court may infer that the accused intended the natural and probable
consequences. Read with Hull — the inferential approach to intent.
• “Serious injury” for s.4 purposes overlaps with but is not identical to “serious harm” in s.1 NFOAP 1997.
In practice the courts treat them similarly.

A.2 Mandatory Life Sentence — s.2 Criminal Justice Act 1990
s.2: A person convicted of murder shall be sentenced to imprisonment for life.
Operational Application
The sentence is mandatory — the trial judge has no discretion as to type. Release is via the Parole Board on
application after the minimum period; under the Parole Act 2019 framework, the process is statutorily
structured.

A.3 Aggravated Murder — s.3 Criminal Justice Act 1990
S.3 sets minimum periods of imprisonment for specified murders — historically including the murder of a
member of An Garda Síochána or prison officer acting in the course of his / her duty, and certain murders in
the course of organised crime.
Operational Application
Where the deceased is a Garda or prison officer killed in the course of duty, the file should record this explicitly
in the cover report. The DPP will draft the indictment with the s.3 aggravating feature in view; the trial judge’s
sentencing remarks address the statutory minimum period.

A.4 Diminished Responsibility — s.6 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006
s.6(1): Where a person is tried for murder and the jury finds that the person did the act alleged, was at the time
suffering from a mental disorder, the mental disorder was not such as to entitle the person to be found not guilty
by reason of insanity, but the mental disorder was such as to diminish substantially his or her responsibility for
the act, the jury shall find the person not guilty of murder but guilty of manslaughter on the ground of diminished
responsibility.
Operational Application

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Partial defence — reduces murder to manslaughter.
• Psychiatric evidence is the central evidential category.
• Preserve mental health history early in the investigation — GP records, hospital records, treatment history.
• Engage with the suspect’s legal representatives where psychiatric evidence is anticipated; the prosecution
may instruct its own expert.

A.5 Insanity (Full Defence) — s.5 Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006
s.5: Where a person is tried for an offence and is found to have been suffering from a mental disorder of such
a nature that the person ought not to be held responsible (he or she did not know the nature and quality of the
act, or did not know it was wrong, or was unable to refrain from committing the act), the special verdict of not
guilty by reason of insanity shall be returned.
Operational Application
Distinct from s.6 diminished responsibility. Insanity is a full defence resulting in special verdict and detention
under mental health framework rather than imprisonment. Mental health Review Board engagement postverdict.

A.6 Self-Defence in the Dwelling — Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011
s.2(1): A person who uses force in defence of his or her dwelling does not commit an offence where (a) the
person honestly believed that an intruder was committing or was about to commit an offence in the dwelling,
and (b) the force used was such as the person reasonably believed necessary in the circumstances as the person
believed them to be.
s.2(4): There is no duty to retreat before using force in defence of the dwelling.
Operational Application
• Applies to use of force in the dwelling — the location of the force is essential. Force at the front door /
inside is within; force outside the boundary may not be.
• Honest belief is subjective; reasonable belief in necessity of force is judged on circumstances as the user
believed them — hybrid test.
• No retreat duty is a significant Irish departure from common-law jurisdictions.
• Investigation: scene location precisely fixed; householder account; threat picture as householder reasonably
saw it; force used analysed against the perceived threat.

Part B — Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997
B.1 Definitions — s.1
Harm: Harm to body or mind, and includes pain and unconsciousness.
Serious harm: Injury that creates a substantial risk of death OR causes serious disfigurement OR causes
substantial loss or impairment of mobility of the body as a whole, or of the function of any particular bodily
member or organ.
Operational Application
• Pain alone = harm (s.1) — sufficient for s.3 NFOAP. Bruising with pain crosses the threshold.
• Serious harm includes serious disfigurement — scarring is included where serious.
• Substantial loss / impairment of function — broken bones with continued functional impact, organ damage,
etc.

B.2 Assault — s.2
s.2(1): A person shall be guilty of the offence of assault who, without lawful excuse, intentionally or recklessly:
(a) directly or indirectly applies force to or causes an impact on the body of another, or (b) causes another to
believe on reasonable grounds that he or she is likely immediately to be subjected to any such force or impact,
without the consent of the other.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Penalty: Class C fine and / or 6 months — summary only.

B.3 Assault Causing Harm — s.3
s.3: A person who assaults another causing him or her harm shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: 5 years on indictment; class A fine / 12 months summary.

B.4 Assault Causing Serious Harm — s.4
s.4: A person who intentionally or recklessly causes serious harm to another shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: Life imprisonment on indictment.
Operational Application
• Note s.4 is drafted as “causes” serious harm — not framed as “assault.” Some debate as to whether assault
is technically an element; in practice the offence is investigated as aggravated assault.
• Mens rea — intentional or reckless (subjective Murray standard) as to causing serious harm. The accused
need not have intended death; intent or recklessness as to serious harm suffices.

B.5 Threats to Kill or Cause Serious Harm — s.5
s.5(1): A person who, without lawful excuse, makes to another a threat, by any means intending the other to
believe it will be carried out, to kill or cause serious harm to that other or a third person shall be guilty of an
offence.
Penalty: 10 years on indictment.

B.6 Syringe Offences — s.6, s.7, s.8
s.6 — Use of syringe: A person who injures another or causes another to believe likely to be injured by use of
a syringe / blood-contaminated article, or threatens with one, shall be guilty of an offence. Higher offence where
intent to cause harm or to intimidate.
Penalty (s.6): Various; up to life where intent to cause serious harm.
s.7 — Possession of syringe: Possession of a syringe / blood-contaminated article with intent to cause harm or
intimidate.
Penalty (s.7): Up to 10 years on indictment.
s.8 — Placing of syringe: Placing of a syringe / blood-contaminated article in a public place creating a risk of
injury.
Penalty (s.8): Up to 5 years on indictment.

B.7 Coercion — s.9
s.9(1): A person who, with a view to compelling another to abstain from doing or to do any act which that other
has a lawful right to do or to abstain from doing, wrongfully and without lawful authority (a) uses violence or
intimidation to that other, his or her family or member of family, or (b) injures or damages his / her property,
or (c) persistently follows, watches, besets the residence / workplace, or follows in a disorderly manner in or
through a public place, shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: 5 years on indictment.

B.8 Harassment — s.10
s.10(1): A person who, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, by any means including by use of
telephone, harasses another by persistently following, watching, pestering, besetting or communicating with
him or her, shall be guilty of an offence.
s.10(2): A person harasses another where (a) he or she, by his or her acts intentionally or recklessly, seriously
interferes with the other’s peace and privacy or causes alarm, distress or harm to the other, and (b) his or her
acts are such that a reasonable person would realise would seriously interfere with the other’s peace and privacy
or cause alarm, distress or harm to the other.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Penalty: Up to 7 years on indictment.
Operational Application
S.10 has two limbs — subjective intent / recklessness (s.10(2)(a)) and objective foreseeability (s.10(2)(b)). Both
required. Charge with s.39 stalking (CJ(MP)A 2023) where the more focused fear / day-to-day impact elements
are met.

B.9 Poisoning — s.12
s.12(1): A person who intentionally or recklessly administers a substance to another, or causes another to take
a substance, which is capable of interfering substantially with the other’s bodily functions, without that other’s
consent.
Penalty: Up to 3 years on indictment.
Operational Application
Where intent to murder accompanies the poisoning, the appropriate charge is attempted murder (common law)
or, where death results, murder. Where intent is to cause serious harm, s.4 NFOAP may also engage.

B.10 Endangerment — s.13
s.13: A person shall be guilty of an offence who intentionally or recklessly engages in conduct which creates a
substantial risk of death or serious harm to another.
Penalty: Up to 7 years on indictment.

B.11 Endangering Traffic — s.14
s.14: Intentional / unlawful conduct creating endangerment of road, rail, marine, or air traffic.
Penalty: Up to 7 years on indictment.

B.12 False Imprisonment — s.15
s.15(1): A person who intentionally or recklessly: (a) takes or detains, or (b) causes to be taken or detained, or
(c) otherwise restricts the personal liberty of another without that other’s consent shall be guilty of the offence
of false imprisonment.
Penalty: Life imprisonment on indictment.

B.13 Abduction of Child — s.16
s.16: A person who unlawfully takes or detains a child under the age of 16 with intent to remove the child from
the lawful control of any person having lawful control of the child.
Penalty: Up to 7 years on indictment.

B.14 Statutory Self-Defence — s.18
s.18(1): The use of force by a person for any of the following purposes, if only such as is reasonable in the
circumstances as he or she believes them to be, does not constitute an offence: (a) to protect himself / herself
or a member of the family from injury, assault, detention, or death caused by a criminal act; (b) to protect the
person from trespass to person; (c) to protect his / her property from criminal damage / trespass / theft; (d) to
protect property of another from same; (e) to prevent crime or breach of the peace.
Operational Application
Operates with Dwyer common-law principles. The statutory restatement does not displace the Dwyer excessive
self-defence partial defence — in murder cases, excessive self-defence remains available as a partial defence
reducing to manslaughter.

Part C — Stalking — s.39 Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act
2023

s.39: A person who, by an act or acts, intentionally or recklessly causes another (a) to fear that violence will be
used against him or her or another person, or (b) serious alarm or distress that has a substantial adverse impact
on his or her usual day-to-day activities, is guilty of stalking.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment.
Operational Application
• New offence introduced 2023; substantial overlap with s.10 NFOAP 1997 harassment.
• Two limbs: (a) fear of violence — either to IP or to a third person; (b) serious alarm / distress + substantial
adverse impact on usual day-to-day activities.
• Substantial adverse impact — requires evidence of changed behaviour, withdrawal from activities, sleep /
work / health impact.
• Where elements met, charge under s.39 in preference to s.10 — the 2023 offence is the more focused and
serious provision.

Part D — Assault on Garda — s.19 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994
s.19(1): Any person who resists or wilfully obstructs or impedes a peace officer acting in the execution of the
peace officer’s duty, or a person assisting a peace officer in the execution of such duty, shall be guilty of an
offence.
s.19(3): Any person who assaults or causes bodily harm to a peace officer acting in the execution of the peace
officer’s duty, or a person assisting a peace officer in the execution of such duty, shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty (s.19(1)): Class A fine / 6 months summary.
Penalty (s.19(3)): Up to 7 years on indictment.
Operational Application
• “Peace officer” includes members of An Garda Síochána, prison officers, members of the Defence Forces
on operational duty; assaults on emergency workers (ambulance, fire) have additional protections under
related legislation.
• “In the execution of duty” is a constitutive element — if the underlying Garda action is unlawful (no
reasonable grounds, no statutory authority, etc.), the offence under s.19(3) is not made out. Investigation
must establish the lawful execution rigorously.

Part E — Custody and Detention
E.1 s.4 Criminal Justice Act 1984 — Detention for Arrestable Offences
Initial period: 6 hours.
Extension by Superintendent: Further 6 hours (total 12).
Extension by Chief Superintendent (if available) / Superintendent depending on regime nuances: Further
12 hours (total 24).
Note: Detention must be necessary for proper investigation; reviews mandatory; rest periods (8pm-8am
suspension for normal-hours sleep) operate within the regime.

E.2 s.50 Criminal Justice Act 2007 — Detention in Organised-Crime Cases
Initial: 24 hours (extensible).
Subsequent: Up to 72 hours initially with subsequent extensions to 168 hours total in stages with senior officer
/ District Court authorisations.
Application: Murder, certain other serious offences with organised-crime feature.

E.3 Inferences from Silence — s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984 (as amended)
Provisions allowing inferences from a suspect’s failure to mention facts later relied on, failure to account for
objects / substances / marks, failure to account for presence at a particular place.

The operational framework for Garda custody: custody record, member-in-charge, rest periods, visits,

Part F — Sentencing
F.1 Parole Act 2019
Restructured the parole regime in Ireland. The Parole Board operates as an independent statutory body. Lifesentence prisoners may apply for parole consideration after the minimum period determined by reference to the
offence and any statutory aggravation (s.3 CJA 1990 minimum periods, etc.).

See Case Law Appendix Part H. The principles: proportionality (Tiernan); structured discretion with starting

Part G — Victims
G.1 Criminal Justice (Victims of Crime) Act 2017
Implements Directive 2012/29/EU. Confers rights on victims of crime:
• Right to information about the criminal justice process.
• Right to translation and interpretation.
• Right to protection from secondary victimisation.
• Right to be notified of suspect / accused custody status, release, or escape.
• Right to make a Victim Impact Statement at sentencing.
• Special measures for vulnerable victims (screens, video link, etc.).
Operational Application
• Victim Information Officer (VIO) / Family Liaison Officer (FLO) at appropriate level for the offence
severity.
• Victim Needs Assessment for serious offences — vulnerable categories (sexual offences, DV, child
victims, victims with disability) receive enhanced provision.
• File should include victim impact material for sentencing stage.

Appendix — Irish Case Law Resource (Vol 2 — Person)
This appendix is the legal backbone of Volume 2. Each Irish authority is treated in depth: Citation, Facts,
Holding, Reasoning, Significance, and Operational Application. ECtHR and CJEU authority is treated more
briefly but with the operational hook clearly identified. The aim is that this appendix alone can be used as a
pre-court refresher, a cover-report drafting tool, or coaching material before contentious decisions.
Verification reminder: case law is alive. Verify status on courts.ie / vLex Ireland before relying in court.

Part A — Homicide Authority (Irish)
A.1 Mens Rea and Intent
The People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360 — Subjective Recklessness
Citation: [1977] IR 360 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The Murray case concerned the killing of a Garda. The accused fired a weapon during the course of the
offence. The question for the court was the level of mens rea required for the capital offence of murder of a
member of An Garda Síochána, in particular whether constructive or objective recklessness was sufficient or
whether subjective awareness was required.
Holding: Recklessness in Irish criminal law is subjective. The accused must have been actually aware of the
risk — not merely whether a reasonable person would have been aware. Constructive recklessness, where the
accused did not personally appreciate the risk but ought to have done so, does not meet the Irish criminal-law
threshold.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court drew the subjective test from the moral foundations of criminal liability — a
person should not be punished as severely as a person who consciously took a risk unless they themselves were
aware of that risk. Walsh J. and Henchy J. delivered reasoning that has structured Irish criminal mens rea
analysis ever since.
Significance: Foundational. Murray is the touchstone Irish authority on the subjective standard of recklessness.
It frames mens rea analysis across the NFOAP suite (s.2, s.3, s.4 assault; s.13 endangerment), and the Cagney
and McGrath reaffirmation of 2008 keeps Murray live.
Operational Application: Whenever recklessness is the live mens rea — typically in s.3 / s.4 NFOAP and s.13
endangerment — investigation should produce evidence of the accused’s subjective awareness of risk: their
words at the time, their conduct showing knowledge of the situation, their experience or training that would put
them on notice, their post-incident conduct showing awareness.
DPP v Hull (1996) Court of Criminal Appeal — Inferred Intent
Citation: Unreported, CCA, 1996; subsequently followed.
Facts: The accused was convicted of murder; the issue on appeal centred on how the jury could properly find
the specific intent of murder (intent to kill or cause serious injury under s.4 CJA 1964) when the accused had
not made an admission of that intent.
Holding: Intent to kill or cause serious injury may be inferred from the nature of the act itself. Relevant
inferential considerations include: the weapon used; the part of the body targeted; the number of blows; the
force of the blows; the accused’s words before, during, and after the act; and the accused’s post-incident
conduct.
Reasoning: The court recognised the realistic limit on prosecution evidence: rarely does an accused declare
their intent. Intent is a mental state and must be inferred from the external manifestations of conduct.
Significance: Hull is the operational guidepost for building a murder case where the accused does not admit
intent. Investigation needs to assemble each component of the inferential picture systematically.
Operational Application: For every potential murder file, document: (a) the weapon and how it was obtained;
(b) location of injuries on deceased — head / chest / abdomen are powerful evidence of intent to cause serious
harm; (c) number and force of blows, informed by pathologist evidence; (d) words spoken before (threats,
planning), during (statements at scene), or after (statements at custody, to family); (e) accused’s conduct —
fleeing, cleaning up, disposing of weapon, going about ordinary life.
