A.2 Provocation — Partial Defence (Murder to Manslaughter)
The People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — Foundational Provocation Test
Citation: [1978] IR 27 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused was convicted of the murder of a man with whom he had been drinking. On the evening of
the killing, the accused alleged the deceased had attacked him with a hammer in a sustained way; the accused
then took the hammer and struck the deceased multiple times, fatally. The trial judge directed the jury in
conventional terms drawing on English authority — specifically the objective “reasonable man” test from
English provocation jurisprudence.
Holding: Provocation in Irish law is a partial defence reducing murder to manslaughter. The test is subjective
— measured by the accused’s own response, not by a hypothetical reasonable person. The accused must have
lost self-control, and the loss of control must have been sudden and temporary, not used as a pretext for a
planned response.
Reasoning: The Court of Criminal Appeal departed deliberately from English authority. The Irish approach
examines whether THIS accused, with their particular characteristics and history, actually lost self-control. The
court rejected imposition of an objective standard that would defeat a defendant whose subjective response,
though real, differed from what a reasonable person would do.
Significance: MacEoin is the foundational Irish authority on provocation. It established a defendant-friendly
subjective test that distinguishes Irish provocation jurisprudence from common-law neighbours. The decision
has structured every subsequent provocation case.
Operational Application: Anticipate provocation in every heat-of-moment killing: domestic confrontations;
pub / street fights; reactions to insult or revelation; killings following sustained or repeated provoking conduct.
Investigation: (a) sequence of events with precise timing; (b) any cooling-off opportunities between provocation
and response; (c) accused’s state — intoxication, fatigue, mental distress; (d) accused’s explanation — did they
describe themselves as having lost control. The prosecution must show response was not the product of sudden
and temporary loss of self-control.
DPP v Davis [2001] 1 IR 146 — Provocation Refined
Citation: [2001] 1 IR 146 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused appealed against a murder conviction, arguing that the trial judge had not properly directed
the jury on provocation, in particular as to the degree of loss of self-control required.
Holding: Re-stated the MacEoin subjective test. Critically, the court held that mere anger or upset is insufficient
— the loss must be of a degree that the accused was not master of his or her actions. The accused must have
actually lost self-control in a meaningful, behavioural sense, not merely have been emotionally aroused.
Reasoning: Davis pushes the analysis from a general subjective standard to a specific behavioural threshold.
Without this refinement, the partial defence could expand to any case where the accused was upset — which
would dilute the specific intent requirement of murder.
Significance: Davis is essential pairing with MacEoin. Practitioners should always cite both: MacEoin for the
subjective framework, Davis for the behavioural threshold.
Operational Application: Interview questions “were you in control of yourself when you did this” and “did
you choose to do this” are evidentially significant. Document the accused’s own account of their level of
control. Defence cases presenting the accused as merely angry, hurt, or jealous — without behavioural loss of
self-control — fall short of Davis.
DPP v McDonagh [2001] 3 IR 411 — Provocation and Trial Direction
Citation: [2001] 3 IR 411 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused was convicted of murder. On appeal, issues arose as to the adequacy of the trial judge’s
directions on provocation, including how the jury should approach the subjective test.
Holding: Reaffirmed the MacEoin / Davis approach. Trial judges must direct juries clearly that the test is
subjective — measured by the accused’s actual response — and must explain what loss of self-control means
in the behavioural sense.
Significance: McDonagh sits in the line of appellate cases requiring full and clear provocation directions
whenever the defence is in play. Inadequate directions are a route to conviction-quashing.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operational Application: Trial direction is not the Garda’s role, but the investigation must build the file so
that a properly directed jury has the material to evaluate the subjective test: detailed timeline; accused’s account
of state of mind; expert evidence (where appropriate) on mental state.
DPP v Heffernan [2017] IESC 5 — Modern Provocation Articulation
Citation: [2017] IESC 5 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The Supreme Court considered modern parameters of the provocation defence in the context of a
sustained provocation scenario and questions about the temporal element.
Holding: Reaffirmed the subjective MacEoin / Davis approach as the modern Irish position. The court
considered nuances of cumulative provocation — where the provoking conduct is sustained over time — and
the interplay with the sudden-and-temporary requirement. The defence remains anchored in subjective loss of
self-control; the temporal element is to be applied with some flexibility to the realities of human emotion.
Significance: Heffernan is the most recent Supreme Court treatment. Confirms the Irish provocation
framework is settled as subjective but explicitly recognises that the application requires sensitivity to sustainedprovocation contexts (relevant to many DV-context homicides where the eventual killer has been the long-term
subject of abuse).
Operational Application: In sustained-provocation cases — typically DV-context killings — do not assume
that earlier acts of provocation are too remote in time. Document the full history. Heffernan opens the door to
consideration of sustained provocation; the investigation should furnish the material for that consideration.

A.3 Self-Defence
The People (AG) v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — Foundational Self-Defence Framework
Citation: [1972] IR 416 (Court of Criminal Appeal / Supreme Court).
Facts: The accused was convicted of the murder of a man during a confrontation in which the deceased had
assaulted the accused. The accused responded with force that the trial court found exceeded the bounds of
reasonable self-defence.
Holding: Self-defence in Irish law has two limbs: (a) the accused must have honestly believed in the need to
use force, and (b) the force used must have been reasonable in the circumstances as the accused believed them
to be. Where the accused honestly believed in the need to use force but used unreasonable force, the partial
defence of “excessive self-defence” operates to reduce murder to manslaughter (but does not provide a complete
acquittal).
Reasoning: The court constructed a hybrid test — subjective on the question of belief, but objective
(“reasonable”) on the question of the amount of force, judged on the circumstances as the accused subjectively
perceived them. The novel contribution was the recognition of excessive self-defence as a partial defence,
mirroring the position in provocation.
Significance: Dwyer is the centrepiece Irish authority on self-defence. The excessive self-defence partial
defence is an Irish innovation that distinguishes Irish law from English. It is now further structured by s.18
NFOAP 1997 and s.2 Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011.
Operational Application: In any case where self-defence is in issue: (a) investigate the IP’s conduct toward
the accused — was there a perceived threat; (b) document the accused’s account of their belief at the time; (c)
measure the force used objectively against the circumstances as the accused subjectively believed them —
weapon used, location of blows, repetition, duration; (d) consider the excessive self-defence partial defence —
a case may be a murder if neither limb met, but a manslaughter if the first limb met and the second not.
DPP v Barnes [2006] IECCA 165 — Defence of the Dwelling Pre-2011 Act
Citation: [2006] IECCA 165 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused, a householder, killed an intruder during a burglary at his home. The case was decided
before the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 came into force and turned on common-law
principles of self-defence and defence of dwelling.
Holding: Defence of the dwelling is a recognised aspect of self-defence in Irish law. A householder is entitled
to use such force as is reasonable in defence of the dwelling and occupants. The court considered the bounds
of reasonableness in the context of a violent intrusion.
Significance: Barnes is the principal pre-2011 Act authority on dwelling defence. It remains relevant for

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

understanding the common-law backdrop against which the 2011 Act was enacted. Many of the Barnes
principles are now codified, but the case helps interpret the statutory framework.
Operational Application: Where a homeowner kills or seriously injures an intruder, the operative legal
framework today is the CL(D&D)A 2011 (no duty to retreat; reasonable belief judged subjectively as to
circumstances). Barnes is useful background. Investigation: scene rigour, intruder identification, householder
account, threat picture as householder reasonably saw it.
Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 — Statutory Framework
Section 2: A person who uses force in defence of the dwelling does not commit an offence where (a) the person
honestly believed an intruder was committing or was about to commit an offence, and (b) the force used was
such as the person reasonably believed necessary in the circumstances as the person believed them to be.
Section 2(4): Express provision that there is no duty to retreat before using force in defence of the dwelling.
Operational Application: Distinguish carefully between common-law self-defence (Dwyer) outside the
dwelling and statutory dwelling-defence under the 2011 Act. The frameworks differ — in particular the absence
of any retreat duty under the 2011 Act. Investigate scene location precisely; the doorstep is the threshold of
distinct regimes.
s.18 NFOAP 1997 — Statutory Self-Defence Restatement
Section 18: Statutory restatement of self-defence — a person does not commit an offence by using such force
as is reasonable in the circumstances as he or she believes them to be: (a) to protect himself / herself or another
from injury, assault, or detention; (b) to protect his or her property; (c) to protect property of another from theft,
criminal damage, or trespassing; (d) to prevent crime or breach of the peace.
Operational Application: Section 18 is the operative statutory framework for self-defence outside the
dwelling. Dwyer common-law principles continue to inform interpretation, particularly the excessive selfdefence partial defence which is preserved alongside s.18.

A.4 Diminished Responsibility
Criminal Law (Insanity) Act 2006 s.6 — Statutory Framework
Section 6: Where a person is tried for murder and is found to have been suffering from a mental disorder at the
time of the killing, and the mental disorder was not such as to entitle the person to the special verdict of not
guilty by reason of insanity, but it did substantially diminish the person’s responsibility for the act, the person
shall be convicted of manslaughter rather than murder.
Significance: S.6 introduced a partial defence of diminished responsibility into Irish law for the first time — a
significant change from the pre-2006 position where only insanity (full defence) was available. The change
brought Irish law into line with most common-law jurisdictions.
Operational Application: Investigation must preserve mental-health history of any homicide suspect: GP
records, psychiatric records, hospital admissions, prior diagnoses. Engage with the suspect’s legal team where
psychiatric assessment is required — the prosecution may also commission its own psychiatric evidence.
Documentation should be such that the issue can be fully and fairly tried.
DPP v Connolly [2003] 2 IR 1 — Diminished Responsibility Application
Citation: [2003] 2 IR 1 (Supreme Court / CCA).
Facts: The accused, who had a history of mental disorder, killed a victim. The defence raised issues around the
accused’s mental responsibility at the time.
Significance: Useful as a marker of the pre-statutory position and the analytical approach to mental disorder in
homicide cases. Post-2006, the operative test is the s.6 statutory test.
Operational Application: Read alongside s.6 of the 2006 Act. Diminished responsibility post-2006 is squarely
statutory; psychiatric evidence is the key evidential category; the prosecution-side preservation of mental health
history is critical.

A.5 Manslaughter — Unlawful Act and Gross Negligence
DPP v Mahalingam [1995] 2 IR 117 — Unlawful Act Manslaughter
Citation: [1995] 2 IR 117 (Court of Criminal Appeal).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Facts: The accused was involved in an altercation that resulted in the death of the IP from a blow. The issue
was whether the accused, in striking the IP, was guilty of murder, manslaughter, or no offence. The mens rea
picture did not support intent to kill or cause serious injury; the question was whether unlawful act manslaughter
applied.
Holding: Unlawful act manslaughter requires: (a) an unlawful act by the accused (in this case the assault); (b)
that act being objectively dangerous in the sense that a sober and reasonable person would recognise that some
harm (not death, not even serious harm) was a risk; (c) the unlawful act causing death. The accused need not
personally have appreciated the risk; it is the objective dangerousness that engages the offence.
Reasoning: The CCA followed the English Church / Newbury / Jones line of authority but situated it firmly in
Irish law. The court was careful to distinguish unlawful act manslaughter (objective dangerousness) from
murder (subjective intent to kill or cause serious injury). The objective standard is justified by the underlying
unlawful act — the accused is already engaged in wrongdoing and must take responsibility for the
consequences.
Significance: Mahalingam is the controlling authority on unlawful act manslaughter. Critical in one-punch
killings, drunken-fight fatalities, and any case where an accused’s assault unexpectedly results in death.
Operational Application: Investigation in one-punch / fight fatalities: (a) establish the unlawful act with full
assault-investigation rigour; (b) establish the objective dangerousness (a punch to the head is widely understood
as dangerous; the prosecution case is usually self-evident on this); (c) establish causation — pathologist
evidence linking the blow to the death; (d) do not get sidetracked by the accused’s subjective non-appreciation;
Mahalingam is objective on this limb.
DPP v Curran [2011] IECCA — Gross Negligence Manslaughter
Citation: [2011] IECCA (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused was a professional whose conduct, alleged to amount to gross negligence, contributed to
the death of a person to whom a duty of care was owed.
Holding: Gross negligence manslaughter requires: (a) a duty of care owed by the accused to the deceased; (b)
breach of that duty; (c) the breach amounting to gross negligence — going beyond ordinary negligence to such
a degree that criminal sanction is justified; (d) the breach creating a serious and obvious risk of death; (e) the
breach causing death. The breach must be of such a degree that the conduct, judged objectively, is appropriately
characterised as criminal.
Reasoning: The court synthesised the Irish position on gross negligence manslaughter drawing on English
authority (Adomako) and Irish common-law principles. The court emphasised that the conduct must be
substantially worse than civil negligence — the criminal sanction is reserved for conduct that is grossly
negligent in a criminal sense.
Significance: Curran is the modern Irish authority on gross negligence manslaughter. It is increasingly relevant
in healthcare, workplace, transport, and care-of-vulnerable-adult cases.
Operational Application: Gross-negligence investigations require: (a) duty-of-care evidence — contract,
statutory role, professional position, relationship; (b) breach evidence — documentation, witness statements,
expert assessment of professional standards; (c) seriousness / obviousness of risk — typically expert evidence;
(d) causation — pathologist plus expert. Cases may run alongside HSA, regulatory, or coronial investigations
— coordination needed.
AG (People) v Cullagh (1961) — Earlier Gross Negligence Authority
Citation: (1961) earlier Irish authority on gross negligence in manslaughter.
Significance: Part of the Irish line of authority that culminates in Curran. Useful for historical perspective on
the development of the offence.

A.6 Joint Enterprise / Common Design
AG (People) v Crosbie and Meehan [1966] IR 490 — Common Design Foundation
Citation: [1966] IR 490 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: Two accused were involved in a confrontation that resulted in the death of the deceased. The question
was whether both accused, even though only one inflicted the fatal blow, could be liable for the resulting offence
by virtue of common design.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Holding: Where two or more persons embark on a common design to commit an unlawful act, each is liable
for the acts of the other in the course of the common design. The scope of liability extends to acts that were
within the common design or that the secondary party foresaw as a possible incident of the common design.
Reasoning: The CCA established the foundational principle that participation in a common design carries
liability for the acts of co-participants within the scope of that design. The doctrine prevents accomplices from
being insulated from the consequences of group action.
Significance: Crosbie and Meehan is the foundational Irish authority on joint enterprise. It pre-dates Reilly and
remains an essential reference.
Operational Application: Multi-suspect homicide / assault investigations require evidence of: (a) the existence
of a common design (planning, communications, agreement); (b) each accused’s participation; (c) the scope of
the design — what was agreed and what was foreseen as a possible incident.
DPP v Reilly [2004] IESC 51 — Modern Joint Enterprise Framework
Citation: [2004] IESC 51 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Multi-accused case where the question of secondary-party liability for the principal’s use of fatal
violence was central.
Holding: Joint enterprise liability requires the accomplice to have foreseen the principal’s use of the means
causing death. The accomplice need not have intended death; foresight of the relevant act suffices for joint
enterprise liability. The scope of the joint enterprise is determined by the parties’ agreement and the foresight
of each.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court refined the joint enterprise doctrine, anchoring it explicitly on the foresight of
the secondary party. The decision aligns Irish law with the general common-law approach while preserving the
structured, foresight-based test.
Significance: Reilly is the modern Irish authority on joint enterprise. Practitioners should always cite Reilly
with Crosbie and Meehan as the foundational background.
Operational Application: Investigation in multi-suspect cases must establish each accused’s foresight
separately. Evidence of foresight includes: communications between accused; planning evidence (acquisition
of weapons, reconnaissance, agreements); each accused’s position and role at scene; post-incident conduct (e.g.,
disposal of evidence). The defence in multi-suspect cases routinely carves out individual clients on foresight
grounds; the prosecution must build the individualised foresight picture.
DPP v Cumberton (Court of Criminal Appeal) — Withdrawal from Joint Enterprise
Facts and Holding: The court considered the doctrine of withdrawal from a joint enterprise — the proposition
that a secondary party who effectively withdraws from the common design before the principal’s act of harm
can avoid liability.
Significance: Withdrawal must be effective, timely, and communicated. Mere mental dissociation is
insufficient. The withdrawing party must take steps to neutralise their previous contribution where possible.
Operational Application: Where any accused claims withdrawal, investigate: what they said / did to
communicate withdrawal; whether they attempted to prevent the offence; whether their previous contributions
were neutralised. The bar is high.

A.7 Causation in Homicide
Causation is rarely set out in a single landmark Irish case but is a constant evidential battleground in homicide.
The principles are: the accused’s act must be a substantial and operative cause of death; pre-existing conditions
(eggshell-skull principle) do not break the chain; medical intervention does not break the chain unless palpably
wrong; intervening acts of the IP themselves (or third parties) may or may not break the chain depending on
the circumstances. The pathologist’s evidence is central; expert evidence may be required on multi-causal
scenarios.
Operational Application
• Pathologist statement on cause of death and mechanism.
• Medical intervention review where complications occurred.
• Pre-existing medical condition assessment via medical history disclosure.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Statement on any intervening third party or IP action.

Part B — Assault Authority (Irish)
DPP v Cagney and McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111 — Recklessness Reaffirmed
Citation: [2008] 2 IR 111 (Supreme Court / Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused were convicted of offences arising from a violent incident. The mens rea question was
central, particularly the standard of recklessness to be applied for the relevant non-fatal offences.
Holding: The subjective standard of recklessness from Murray was reaffirmed. The accused must have
personally been aware of the risk. The court considered the standard in the specific context of the NFOAP and
confirmed Murray’s continued application.
Significance: Cagney and McGrath is the modern Supreme Court reaffirmation of Murray for the NFOAP
suite. It removes any ambiguity about whether Murray subjective recklessness applies to s.2, s.3, s.4 (where
recklessness is the alternative mens rea limb) and s.13 endangerment.
Operational Application: The accused’s subjective awareness of the risk must be evidenced. In any recklessassault case, the investigation should produce material showing what the accused knew or appreciated at the
relevant time. In interview, probe the accused’s awareness of the situation.
DPP v Murphy [2005] IECCA — Direct and Indirect Force
Citation: [2005] IECCA (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: Case involving an assault in which force was applied indirectly — not by direct blow but by mechanism.
Holding: Force in s.2 NFOAP 1997 may be applied directly (a blow) or indirectly (setting an animal on a
person, pulling a chair away as a person sits, throwing an object, contaminating food). The accused’s act need
not be the immediate cause of the contact; an intermediate cause does not necessarily break the chain.
Significance: Murphy expands the operational scope of assault. Many real-world incidents that might appear
non-assault fit s.2 once Murphy is properly understood.
Operational Application: Indirect-force scenarios fall within s.2 NFOAP. Examples: setting a dog on a person;
throwing a brick; pulling a chair as a person sits; spraying liquid; contaminating food or drink (overlaps with
poisoning s.12); using a vehicle as a means of contact. Investigation: identify the mechanism by which force
was applied; the chain of causation from accused’s act to contact; the accused’s intent / recklessness.
R v Brown [1994] 1 AC 212 (House of Lords; Persuasive in Ireland) — Consent Defence Narrow
English authority cited in Ireland for the proposition that consent is not a defence to actual bodily harm or above
outside recognised exceptions (organised sport, surgical procedures, body modification within limits, certain
religious practices). The Irish position broadly aligns with Brown. In practical terms: a suspect who claims the
IP consented to violent treatment in a non-recognised context will face an uphill argument. Operationally
significant in DV cases where the suspect may claim consensual roughness.
DPP v Slattery [2010] IECCA — Assault on Garda — Force in Arrest
Facts and Holding: Case considering the lawful execution of duty element in s.19 CJ(PO)A 1994. The court
considered the relationship between the lawfulness of the underlying Garda action and the assault charge.
Significance: Confirms that the lawfulness of the underlying Garda action is an element of s.19(3). If the arrest
/ detention / search was not lawful, the assault charge under s.19(3) fails (though other assault charges may
stand).
Operational Application: In every s.19(3) file, document the underlying Garda action grounds in detail. The
notebook entry establishing reasonable suspicion / authority for the action is the foundation of the s.19 charge.

Part C — Threats, Coercion, Harassment, False Imprisonment
DPP v Cully [2016] IECA 240 — Harassment Pattern
Citation: [2016] IECA 240 (Court of Appeal).
Facts: The accused was convicted of harassment under s.10 NFOAP 1997. The case concerned the constituent
elements, particularly the course-of-conduct requirement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Holding: Harassment under s.10 requires a course of conduct — multiple acts forming a pattern. Single isolated
acts do not constitute s.10 harassment. The pattern itself is the offence; each individual act may be lawful but
together amount to harassment.
Reasoning: The Court of Appeal emphasised the persistent / patterned nature of harassment as distinct from
one-off offences. The protection of peace and privacy is afforded specifically against sustained interference.
Significance: Cully clarifies the threshold for s.10 — and by extension informs charging decisions between
s.10 (course of conduct) and other offences (threats s.5 — single threat may suffice; coercion s.9 — may be
single or multiple; stalking s.39 CJ(MP)A 2023 — acts or single intense act).
Operational Application: Build the chronological evidence picture for harassment cases. Use of IP diary going
forward (post-report) and reconstruction backwards. Each act dated and described. Pattern documented across
communications, sightings, third-party reports. Single-act cases require alternative charges.
The People (DPP) v Conroy [1993] 2 IR 305 — Restriction of Liberty
Citation: [1993] 2 IR 305 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: Case involving the false imprisonment offence under s.15 NFOAP 1997 (and its pre-1997 common-law
equivalent). The court considered the threshold of restriction of liberty required to engage the offence.
Holding: Any meaningful restriction on personal liberty without consent suffices for false imprisonment. The
threshold is relatively low. The offence covers any unlawful detention or restraint, not merely formal
confinement.
Reasoning: The court took an expansive view of the protected interest — personal liberty — and confirmed
that the offence reaches a wide range of conduct from formal confinement to lesser but still meaningful
restraints (e.g., blocking exits, taking keys, using fear to prevent leaving).
Significance: Conroy is the operational scope-setting case for s.15. The offence is wider than common
understanding of “kidnap” — it reaches DV detention, coercive-control restraint, group restraint at scenes,
refusal to allow IP to leave a place.
Operational Application: Recognise s.15 in a wider range of cases than instinct suggests. Tiger robberies, DV
detention, coercive control, gang-style abduction, even some intimate-partner scenarios where IP was prevented
from leaving. The investigation establishes: the restriction (physical or by fear); the IP’s lack of consent; the
accused’s intent / recklessness.
DPP v Kavanagh [2008] IECCA — False Imprisonment and Robbery
Facts and Holding: Case involving a tiger robbery scenario in which false imprisonment of family members
was used to facilitate the underlying robbery. The court confirmed that false imprisonment and robbery may
both be charged where the elements of each are met.
Significance: Operationally important for tiger robbery cases — do not under-charge by treating the false
imprisonment as ancillary; both offences should be charged where the elements are made out.

Part D — Identification (Casey Warning)
The People (AG) v Casey (No 2) [1963] IR 33 — Visual Identification Warning
Citation: [1963] IR 33 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The accused was convicted on the basis of visual identification evidence. The CCA considered the
dangers of identification evidence and the appropriate warning to be given to a jury where the prosecution case
rested wholly or substantially on visual identification.
Holding: Where the case depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the trial judge must give the
jury a specific warning about the dangers of relying on visual identification: that honest and convinced
witnesses can be mistaken; that previous reliability of the witness in other respects does not eliminate this risk;
that the jury must consider the circumstances of the identification (lighting, distance, duration of observation,
prior familiarity).
Reasoning: The court recognised the well-documented psychological reality that visual identification is
unreliable in ways that lay juries may not appreciate. The warning is a structural protection against wrongful
conviction.
Significance: The Casey warning is mandatory where identification is the substantial basis of the prosecution

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

case. Failure to give it is grounds for quashing conviction. Casey shapes investigation: identification procedures
matter.
Operational Application: Investigation involving visual identification: (a) document the conditions of the
original sighting (lighting, distance, duration, angle); (b) where formal identification procedures are needed,
use VIPER — Video Identification Parade Electronic Recording — rather than informal procedures; (c)
ADVOKATE mnemonic for assessing the witness sighting: Amount of time, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction,
Knowledge of suspect, Any reason to remember, Time lapse, Errors / discrepancies; (d) avoid leading
procedures and contaminating subsequent witness accounts.
DPP v Maguire [1995] 2 IR 286 — Casey Warning Extension
Facts and Holding: The Supreme Court considered the extension of the Casey warning into related
identification contexts and reaffirmed the requirement of clear warnings in identification-dependent cases.
Significance: Maguire keeps the Casey warning alive in modern practice and reinforces its mandatory
application.

Part E — Custody, Interview, Admissibility
The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 — Voluntariness of Admissions
Citation: [1977] IR 336 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The accused was convicted in part on the basis of admissions made during custody. The defence
challenged the admissibility of those admissions on voluntariness grounds.
Holding: Admissions are admissible only if voluntary — not obtained by oppression, threat, inducement, or
other circumstance impugning free choice. The prosecution bears the burden of proving voluntariness beyond
reasonable doubt.
Significance: Madden is the foundational Irish authority on voluntariness. It anchors every admissibility
challenge in custody-derived evidence.
Operational Application: Every interview producing admissions must be supported by evidence of
voluntariness: caution properly administered; suspect’s capacity assured; lawyer access vindicated; no
oppression / inducement; rest, refreshment, medical assessment as needed; audio / video recording where
applicable; custody record contemporaneous and detailed.
DPP v Healy [1990] 2 IR 73 — Solicitor Access Foundation
Citation: [1990] 2 IR 73 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The accused was interviewed in custody. The question of access to a solicitor and the consequences of
denial / delay arose.
Holding: A person in Garda custody has a constitutional right of reasonable access to a solicitor. Denial of this
right or unreasonable delay in vindicating it may render subsequent admissions inadmissible. The right is rooted
in the Constitution (Article 38, fair trial).
Reasoning: The Supreme Court located the right in constitutional fair-trial guarantees, not merely in the
Custody Regulations. The right is constitutional in nature and therefore commands robust protection.
Significance: Healy is the foundation of Irish solicitor-access jurisprudence. It pre-dates the modern Gormley
/ Doyle / Salduz line but laid the constitutional groundwork.
Operational Application: Solicitor access is a constitutional right. Every reasonable step must be taken to
vindicate it without delay. Documentation of every step — notification, contact attempts, solicitor arrival,
consultation, return to interview — is essential.
DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 — Pre-Interview Solicitor Access
Citation: [2014] IESC 17 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The Supreme Court considered whether interviews could be conducted before the suspect had access to
legal advice. The cases involved separate accused (Gormley and White) whose admissibility challenges turned
on this point.
Holding: A suspect has a right to consult a solicitor before substantive questioning in Garda custody. Interviews
conducted before this consultation has been afforded (or where the suspect has not waived the right) engage

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

admissibility concerns.
Reasoning: The court synthesised the Healy constitutional foundation with the ECtHR Salduz / Beuze
trajectory. The court placed lawyer access at the front of the custody process rather than as a peripheral
consideration.
Significance: Gormley shifts the operational tempo of custody: substantive questioning waits for legal
consultation. The decision has fundamentally reshaped Garda interview practice in serious cases.
Operational Application: In serious-offence custody (including all person-crime offences at s.3 / s.4 NFOAP
and above), solicitor consultation precedes substantive interview unless waived by the suspect. Documentation:
solicitor notification, arrival, consultation period, suspect waiver where applicable.
DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 — Solicitor Presence During Interview
Citation: [2017] IESC 1 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The Supreme Court considered whether a suspect has a right to have a solicitor physically present during
interview, beyond the consultation right vindicated in Gormley.
Holding: The Supreme Court declined to extend Gormley to a constitutional right of solicitor presence during
interview (as distinct from prior consultation). The court considered ECtHR Beuze and other authority but did
not extend the Irish position to the Strasbourg high-water mark.
Reasoning: The court balanced fair-trial interests against the operational realities of custody investigation. The
constitutional right is one of consultation; presence in interview is not constitutionally required, though it may
be facilitated.
Significance: Doyle marks the current Irish position. The Strasbourg trajectory (Beuze) may yet push Irish law
further; for now, Gormley consultation pre-interview is the standard, with solicitor presence during interview
facilitated where requested but not constitutionally required.
Operational Application: Comply with Gormley pre-interview consultation rigorously. Facilitate solicitor
presence in interview where requested — it is increasingly common practice. Document all interactions.
DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 — Modern Exclusionary Rule
Citation: [2015] IESC 31 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The case concerned evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights and the question of admissibility.
The Supreme Court considered whether the strict exclusionary rule of People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110
should be modified.
Holding: The court modified the Kenny rule. Where evidence is obtained in breach of constitutional rights, it
is presumptively inadmissible, but the breach may be excused where it was inadvertent and excusable.
Deliberate or conscious violation results in exclusion; inadvertent breach by an officer acting in good faith may
not.
Reasoning: The court balanced the deterrent / rights-protective function of exclusion against the cost of
excluding probative evidence in cases of accidental breach. The good-faith exception is constrained — it does
not licence carelessness.
Significance: JC is the modern controlling authority on the exclusionary rule. It moderates Kenny but does not
abolish exclusion as a primary remedy. Procedural rigour remains operationally essential.
Operational Application: Comply with constitutional / statutory requirements rigorously. JC offers a margin
for inadvertent breach in good faith but not for systemic carelessness. Documentation of grounds for action,
authorisations, procedural steps — all are essential.
DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — Search Warrant Validity
Citation: [2012] IESC 11 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The case concerned the constitutional validity of search warrant procedures, specifically whether a
member of the same investigation could issue the warrant.
Holding: Search warrants must be issued by an independent person not engaged in the investigation. Warrants
issued by an officer of the same investigative team breach constitutional requirements of independence.
Significance: Damache restructured search warrant procedure. Investigations must use independent District
Court / Peace Commissioner / authorised officer routes; in-house warrants are unconstitutional.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operational Application: Always route search warrants through independent issuing authorities. Document
the issuing authority’s independence on the file.
DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 — Adverse Inferences from Silence
Citation: [2002] 2 IR 268 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: The case considered the operation of adverse-inference provisions in Irish law, particularly the
prerequisites for inferences from silence to be drawn.
Holding: Adverse inferences from silence under the CJA 1984 (as amended by CJA 2007) may only be drawn
where the statutory prerequisites are met: clear caution / warning in the prescribed form; opportunity for legal
consultation; questioning on specific matters within the statutory scope. Inferences cannot be drawn where
prerequisites are not strictly observed.
Significance: Buck enforces strict compliance with the inference provisions. Failure to comply with
prerequisites neutralises the inference at trial.
Operational Application: Inference warnings (s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984) require exact wording and full
procedural compliance. Practitioners should follow approved scripts.
DPP v Walsh [1980] IR 294 — Reasonable Grounds for Arrest
Carried from Vol 1. An arrest must be founded on reasonable suspicion in the mind of the arresting member.
The suspicion must be objectively assessable — capable of being articulated to a court. Operationally critical
in s.19 CJ(PO)A 1994 prosecutions, where the lawfulness of the underlying arrest is an element of the offence.

Part F — European Court of Human Rights
ECtHR authority operates in Irish criminal law principally via Article 2 (right to life — procedural obligation
in death investigation), Article 3 (prohibition of inhuman / degrading treatment), Article 5 (liberty), Article 6
(fair trial — lawyer access, silence, evidence), and Article 8 (privacy).
McCann v UK (1995) 21 EHRR 97 — Article 2 Procedural Obligation
Foundational. Established that Article 2 imposes a positive procedural obligation on the State to investigate
deaths effectively, independently, promptly, and with public scrutiny.
Jordan v UK (2001) 37 EHRR 2 / Finucane v UK (2003) 37 EHRR 29
Reinforce the Article 2 procedural standards. Operationally critical in homicide — the SIO regime is
constructed to meet these standards.
Opuz v Turkey (2009) 50 EHRR 28
Articles 2 and 3 read with Article 14: State has positive obligation to protect victims of DV. Failure to act on
credible warnings is a Convention breach. Operationally critical in DV-context person-crime.
Kontrová v Slovakia (2007) 47 EHRR 13
Reinforces Opuz. Failure to act on DV reports preceding murder = Convention breach. Garda decision-making
in DV is held to Convention standard.
Salduz v Turkey [GC] 27 November 2008 / Beuze v Belgium [GC] 9 November 2018
Custody lawyer access. Salduz is the foundational ECtHR authority; Beuze is the modern restatement. Irish law
via Gormley / Doyle is broadly compliant; the Strasbourg position has historically run ahead of Irish.
Murray (John) v UK 8 February 1996
Inferences from silence permissible only with appropriate safeguards, including lawyer access. Anchors the
procedural prerequisites that DPP v Buck enforces in Irish law.
Saunders v UK 17 December 1996 / Funke v France 25 February 1993
Privilege against self-incrimination. Engages in regulatory cross-evidence cases and compelled-documents
scenarios.
Allan v UK 5 November 2002

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Cell informants / undercover questioning. Material obtained in covert interaction with detained persons engages
Article 6.
Bykov v Russia [GC] 10 March 2009 / Khan v UK 12 May 2000
Surveillance. Material obtained by surveillance must comply with Convention standards — lawfulness,
necessity, proportionality. Irish CJ(Surveillance) Act 2009 framework operates against this Strasbourg
backdrop.

Part G — Court of Justice of the European Union
Directive 2012/29/EU — Victims’ Rights
Implemented in Ireland by the Criminal Justice (Victims of Crime) Act 2017. Confers rights to information,
protection, support, and participation in criminal proceedings. Engaged in every person-crime investigation:
victim information protocol; needs assessment; protection measures; family rights in homicide cases.
Joined Cases C-203/15 and C-698/15 Tele2 Sverige and Watson [2016]
Data retention / access. CJEU restricts general data retention; access by authorities must be limited to serious
crime, prior independent review, etc. Operationally relevant where Garda investigation involves access to
communications data — the framework is constrained.

Part H — Sentencing Principles for Person Crime
Investigation feeds into sentencing through the antecedent statement, victim impact statement, and aggravating
/ mitigating evidence built into the file. The investigating Garda should be familiar with the sentencing
framework even though sentencing is judicial.
The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250 — Sentencing Proportionality
Citation: [1988] IR 250 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Sentencing appeal in a serious offence; principles of proportionality were articulated.
Holding: Sentences must be proportionate to the offence and the offender. The court must consider both the
gravity of the offence and the personal circumstances of the offender. Mitigation is a matter of substance, not
merely formality.
Significance: Tiernan is the foundational Irish authority on sentencing proportionality. It frames the structureddiscretion approach in Irish sentencing.
The People (DPP) v M [1994] 3 IR 306 — Sentencing Guidance
Citation: [1994] 3 IR 306 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Significance: Provided guidance on the structured approach to sentencing in serious offences — starting points,
aggravating and mitigating factors, the proportionality assessment.
DPP v Fitzgibbon [2014] IECCA 12 — Assault Sentencing
Significance: Recent CCA guidance on sentencing in assault offences — weighing the gravity of harm, the
manner of the offence, the offender’s circumstances, prior history, mitigation.
DPP v Princs [2007] IECCA — s.4 NFOAP Sentencing
Significance: Guidance on the range of sentences for s.4 NFOAP serious-harm offences. The starting point and
the structured discretion approach.
Mandatory Life for Murder — Sentencing Framework
Murder carries a mandatory life sentence (s.2 Criminal Justice Act 1990). The Parole Board considers release
after a minimum period; recent statutory framework via the Parole Act 2019 has restructured the process. The
trial judge sentences but has no discretion as to the type of sentence. Aggravating features (e.g., murder of a
Garda — minimum periods may apply by statute) and the parole-process eventual review are the operational
sentencing-end realities.
Manslaughter Sentencing

Discretionary life. Sentencing varies widely depending on the form of manslaughter (voluntary with
provocation tending toward lower; gross negligence varies; unlawful act often 6–12 years; reduced where strong
mitigation).

Part I — Using This Appendix
Pre-Court Refresher
Before any serious court appearance in a person-crime case, read the relevant Part: Part A for homicide; Part B
for assault; Part C for the threats / harassment continuum; Part D for any identification-dependent case; Part E
for any case where custody-derived evidence is in issue.
Cover Report Drafting
Cite the lead Irish authority by name in cover reports. “Dwyer self-defence considered — see para [x]” carries
more weight than “self-defence was considered.”
Coaching and Mentoring
Use the appendix as the legal-end discussion ground for probationer / mentee briefings. The cases here represent
the working knowledge a serious-crime investigator should carry.
Sanity Check Before Contentious Decisions
Before any contentious decision — charge tier, arrest, search, custody extension, evidence handling — verify
against the relevant Irish authority. The appendix is the first reference; courts.ie / vLex Ireland the verification.
Final verification reminder: case law is alive. Verify status before relying.

The Two-Track Operational Framework — Public Order
Public order incidents are predominantly dynamic. The Two-Track framework introduced in Volumes 1 and 2
adapts as follows:

Track A — Live Incident (Dynamic Phase)
Officer safety and IP safety paramount.
Containment of the incident — prevent spread, isolate the group.
Identification of principals — the leader, the violent, the isolated trouble-maker.
Threshold judgment — is the situation a s.5 disorderly, a s.6 threatening, a s.16 affray, a s.15 violent disorder,
a s.14 riot.
Documentation capacity — body-worn video activation, witness identification, scene capture.

Track B — Post-Incident Investigation
BWV review and clip extraction.
CCTV recovery from surrounding premises.
Witness identification and statement-taking — IPs, third parties, licensed-premises staff.
Suspect identification (custody photograph / DNA where appropriate).
Charge selection from the spectrum of POA offences.
File preparation for District Court (most POA matters) or Circuit Court (group disorder, blackmail).

Volume 3 Core Mnemonics
SCENE — Public Order Dynamic Response
S — Safety (self, colleague, IP, public).
C — Containment (prevent spread, isolate).
E — Evidence (BWV on, witnesses noted, CCTV identified).
N — Numbers (identify principals, count group).
E — Engage / Escalate (verbal de-escalation; arrest where threshold met).

PLACE — Public Place Threshold Check
P — Public (or place of public resort) at the material time.
L — Licensed premises (covered as public place by statutory extension).
A — Adjacent open areas (e.g., car parks, doorways forming part of public space).
C — Common parts of multi-occupancy buildings (sometimes covered).
E — Excluded spaces (private dwellings, private workplaces — NOT public).

RGB — Affray / Violent Disorder / Riot Differentiator
R — Riot: 12+ persons, common purpose, use or threat of unlawful violence, person of reasonable firmness
fearful.
V (G) — Violent Disorder: 3+ persons, use or threat of unlawful violence, person of reasonable firmness fearful.
A (B) — Affray: 2+ persons, use or threat of unlawful violence, person of reasonable firmness fearful (no
common purpose required, though typically present).

MAD — Move-On Direction Compliance Check
M — Member in uniform giving the direction.
A — Authority — s.8 CJ(PO)A 1994 grounds met (objects of s.8 — reasonable apprehension of breach of
peace etc.).
D — Direction clear, given properly, recorded, with reasonable opportunity to comply.

Garda Code of Ethics — Public Order Application
Public order policing is the most visible Garda function. It is where the public-trust relationship is most often
tested in real time. The Garda Code of Ethics applies throughout, with three standards of heightened salience:

Standard 1 — Duty
The fundamental Garda duty to protect life, property, and the peace. In public order, this manifests as: positive
intervention where necessary; balanced and proportionate response; recognition that the duty extends to the
rights of all parties — including those engaged in disorder.

Standard 4 — Use of Force
Force used by An Garda Síochána must be lawful, necessary, proportionate, and reasonable. In public order
context, this is the single most operationally tested standard. Force decisions must be: justifiable in the situation
as the member reasonably believed it; restrained where verbal direction would suffice; proportionate to the
lawful objective.

Standard 6 — Impartiality
Public order incidents engage strong opinions — about the participants, about the social context, about the
political content (in protest cases). Standard 6 requires impartial decision-making: the same response to like
conduct regardless of the personal characteristics of those involved; the same threshold for arrest and charge
across the population.

Standard 7 — Speaking Up
Where colleagues engage in conduct that falls below standard — unnecessary force, disrespectful language,
discriminatory treatment — the duty to speak up applies. Public order incidents often involve multiple Gardaí
and there are corresponding multiple opportunities for falling short. The duty operates within Garda procedures
(formal channels available).

The Five Public-Order Ethics Trip-Wires

Compliments — Public Order Categories
The categories of compliment continue from Volumes 1 and 2:
Written compliment from supervisor or member-in-charge — for routine excellence (effective de-escalation,
calm handling, public liaison).
Formal commendation — for distinguished conduct (saving life, exceptional bravery, sustained excellence on
a complex operation).
Peer acknowledgment — colleague recognition for support, learning, mentoring.
Welfare-side recognition — acknowledging the toll of difficult duty (multiple incidents in a night, sustained
operations, witnessing serious injury or death).

Public Order — The Public Visibility Dimension
Public order incidents are commonly recorded by the public, journalists, and bystanders. Gardaí should expect
that conduct may be reviewed publicly later. This expectation is not a restraint on lawful action but a discipline
on conduct: act in a manner the Garda member would be content to defend at all stages.
